For all those whom are concerned and wondering about why this young man has a tiger is, because he rescued Romeo from being sold to the circus. Romeo, the tiger I do believe will be given sanctuary once he grows bigger into his adult stage and be with his own kind. He is not a pet, but this young man considers him family as well as Romeo. Romeo thinks of him as a parental figure and family. The day they have to part will be devastating, but once he adjusts to his new environment it will be for the best. Of course this young man will visit when he can and the reunions will be amazing. Obviously, owning tigers are illegal unless you are known as a rescuer, who rehabilitates and gives sanctuary to certain types of animals for a period of time then transfers them to other qualified well known animal sanctuaries that have the money to do so. 

All in said, please support your local, well-known, and national sanctuaries and rescue places, because it helps their mission to feed, supply medical care, daily care, rescue more animals, advertise, and raise awareness world-wide even local. Thank you!

video_title: That moment You realise You cannot outrun that tiger !
video_description: There is no way of running away from a tiger not even with crooked feet !
text_content:
For anyone who didn't know, this is actually very cat-like behavior even with house cats. Most people don't do it with their cats, anecdotally speaking at least, but with two of the three cats I've had I've basically played "tag" with them. If they seem like they got the zoomies or just feel like playing, I'll sometimes chase them down the hallway in the upstairs of our house. I stop short of the bedroom at the end of the hall while they'd dart in and usually around a corner for "safety." I'd give them a second, and then either peek in or make a slightly exaggerated amount of noise as I start to run away and before I know it, cat's gunning it back at me. I run into my room, turn around, chase him back and repeat. Then there's the more common game that can be mixed in of peering around corners at each other and them closing the distance in between your peeks.

My cat did scare the shit out of me one time during a rare first floor play session running between the living room and kitchen. I just wanted to do a round or two, so after I chased him to the kitchen, I walked part of the way to the stairs and then kinda went up them quickly. I hear something and look behind me and the mfer is gunning for me up the fucking stairs and so I'm like whoa there and go faster and he kept chasing me all the way into my room and I'm like this mfer is gonna cut me holy shit. 

Obviously I died after that.

text_content: What Jon says here confirms what I have heard from other sources.  Setting aside the problem of how to ethically purchase a healthy cheetah cub, the biggest challenge with maintaining a healthy cheetah in captivity is properly sourcing their diet.  If cheetahs do not receive the proper mix of proteins and other nutrients, they will experience various chronic health problems and experience shortened life spans.  From a personality and instinct perspective, cheetahs are the easiest big cat to adapt to a human environment.  Far easier in fact than tigers, lions, leopards, jaguars or pumas, who are potentially very dangerous even when hand-raised from infancy by their humans.  If socialized from cubs to interact with humans, cheetahs will bond and imprint on their human family, and interact like an overgrown house cat.  That said, they instinctually want to mark their territory -- and a 75- to 125-pount cheetah can produce a lot of urine.  They also want to claw and chew inanimate objects.  These are not "indoor" cats; they need to be kept outside a human home, with access to appropriate open space for exercise and play.  At best, having a pet cheetah is an expensive luxury that requires a significant input of cash, time and effort that is beyond the reach of most human beings to provide.

text_content: I recommend gentle dominance with cats.  Most felines don’t comprehend punishment very well and usually will listen out of fear or be anti-social.  If a cat is being persistently hazardous and/ or destructive, I usually pick them up by supporting under the chest with one hand and gently picking them up by the scruff of their neck and relocating.  I try to use physical behavior only when necessary though and calling their name or yelling is usually the first resort.  I’ve met a lot of very aggressive animals in my life who were abused but if you give them space and occasional treats they’ll usually open up to you on their own, just don’t rush it and move slow and in sight if it’s a particularly antisocial animal.  Be calm and relaxed but also prepared to pull away if any defensive aggression is displayed towards you.  Dominance discipline is usually not needed because the right behavior will display dominance.  When you provide food, set boundaries, and occupy your territory, a smaller animal can usually sense you’re the boss unless you act too enabling

text_content:
I used to have a bear cub. Yea sounds crazy, and it was crazy. We never kept him in the house, he was free to go anywhere whenever he pleased. 
He came to our door everynight, and stayed near our house to visit us most times we were outside. We assumed his mother had died because we never saw her. So we fed him. It got the the point where we would pet him and play with him like he was a dog. 
Months pass and he had become family to us. After a few days of not seeing him, we found out he had been shot and killed through facebook.
The man who killed him saw him come near our house and thought he was like any other bear. He wasnt a pet. He was a medium size male black bear. 
I dont remember what we used to call him. I was a kid so I know I had to have given him a name at some point. All I remember is that he LOVED honey buns.
Wild animals can be tamed, not domesticated - and nobody should take them from their natural environment. Even small friendly interactions can impact their wellbeing. ❤❤

text_content:
I had a cat once that heard me throwing up in the toilet, I heard him calling to me running through the house to find me, he came into the bathroom, walked slowly up to me then reached out and placed his paw on me until it was over. 

We were close before this, but after that, I never loved an animal more.

Recently, I almost ran over a kitten on a dirt road in the country. Dark was coming, and it had rained all day. He was so sick, vet said he had a bad fever, he's better now and I can tell we will be just as close as I was with my old buddy. Named him Lucky, seemed appropriate since he was missed being ran over by all the  cars, trucks on that road.

Animals are the closest thing to angels on earth. They are pure and will just return the love you give them.

video_title: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? 🐒 Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? 🙉
video_description: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? As one can imagine, it's easy to fall for their cute faces ...
text_content:
No.  Monkeys - in general - do NOT make "good" pets.

Unless you are very well-prepared and aware of what monkeys are and do, don't get a monkey. They are too intelligent, curious, and possessive to be a good room mate. They will shit and piss anywhere, and at any time.  You cannot physically discipline a monkey, nor is it easy to do so by restricting them from activity or out of cage freedoms. They will bear a grudge. They will bite if you attempt to lift it off the couch, and throw their shit at you as you pass the cage.

This is why they end up mean and vicious, and needing rescue.

DON'T get a monkey.

He was stationed in the tropics at the time, maybe Thailand? I think when he was given orders to leave he gave the monkey to a buddy of his. That was how he acquired the monkey. You really have to know what you are getting yourself into and be prepared to care for this little "human" for the rest of your life. They have hands, emotions, dietary restrictions, restrictions in the states, they want to be with you 24/7, need diapers, can't be baraccaded while you go to work or have a social life. They are like human babies. Extremely needy and they get into everything. Cute as a button, but really think about it for a long time and do research. A lot of research. Are you willing to give up your life for this little one because that is the sacrifice, including where you will live and your monetary budget. ❤❤

text_content:
Slow loris
"Their huge round eyes, adorable faces and almost human-like paws have made slow lorises one of the internet's favourite animals. Being nocturnal, the little primates seem almost docile during the day: when they do move around, they do so slowly and deliberately. When threatened, they tend to freeze.
But slow lorises also have a lethal ace up their sleeve, almost literally.
In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death
When threatened, a loris will often raise its arms above its head. Far from being a sign of surrender, this is a defensive posture, bringing a gland around its elbow close to its mouth.
By combining the oil secreted from the gland with saliva, the loris creates a strong venom, which it then smears onto the top of its head, making it a toxic morsel for any predator.
The loris can also keep the venom inside its mouth, delivering it in a bite. The incisors at the front of the bottom jaw help to conduct the liquid upwards, ensuring it gets into any wound it can inflict.
According to Anne-Isola Nekaris of Oxford Brookes University, a bite from a slow loris is "intensely painful". "In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death," Nekaris says.
There are nine known species of slow loris living in the forests of South East Asia. The venom of the pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus) has been found to contain 220 compounds.
So, the next time you watch a video of a slow loris being "tickled" with its arms in the air, remember it is not enjoying the experience: it is waiting for a chance to strike."

video_title: Bush Baby: The Pet Everyone Wants
video_description: Bush babies are popular on social media but can they be pets? More Information: ...
text_content:
Bushbaby as pets?
The tiny "Nagapie" (night monkey in Afrikaans) are at risk due to humans wanting to keep them as inappropriate pets. 
 
Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

Cute and cuddly looking but did you know they mark their territories by urinating into a cupped hand and then rubbing this on the feet, bushbabies spread their scent wherever they move.  They are also only active at night and need a very specialised diet.
​
Many species of nocturnal primates are increasingly threatened by illegal collecting for the pet trade. When transported outside of their natural range and climate, many bushbabies die. This is partly because they are adapted to a specific climate and landscape, but also because each species has a very specific diet, which they typically do not receive when they are kept as pets, by inexperienced keepers.
 
Furthermore the incorrect diet will result in mal-formed bones, kidney and liver damage, obesity and psychological impairments.
​
Bushbabies are very prone to respiratory problems if the correct temperatures are not maintained.
 
New owners often grow tired quickly; these are nocturnal primates and owners often get bored of them sleeping during the day and do not give enough gum or  adequet insects needed to sustain them at night.  When the novelty wears off the animals are often dumped in the nearest veld. Where they often do not survive as they do not know the landscape; dehydration and starvation.
​
It is AGAINST THE LAW TO KEEP INDIGENOUS WILD ANIMALS AS PETS AND UNETHICAL.
​
If you see someone with an indigenous wild animals as a pet, get in touch.  Or call your local SPCA.

text_content: Bless your heart for rescuing all these animals. I did what one should never do. I bought an exotic pet without doing any research first because it was a baby and adorable. That pet was an Umbrella or Great White Cockatoo. Fortunately I was willing to put in the work and learn everything I could about them and how to care for them properly. Parrots of all kinds, but especially the different cockatoos, are a lot of work. You can’t just put them in a cage and feed them seeds. If you do you get a very socially unadjusted and sick bird. After all this research and putting into practice what I had learned with my parrot I began to look around and see all the parrots that were being neglected, abused and abandoned because people didn’t realize how much work they were, how long they lived (they are a lifetime pet) and the novelty of owning one had worn off. I now rescue parrots most of whom are a cockatoo of some sort. Cockatoos are very social and needy birds which is why they are the most abused and abandoned parrots out there. You however rescue so many different kinds of animals that require such different and specialized care. My hat is off to you. Well done.

text_content: I have had my AG since he was a baby, 25 yrs now and I love him to much.  Max is so funny, does all the bird things and he is my door bell.  No one can come to the door without him letting out a very loud high pitched whistle.  He does not take to anyone but me.  One thing I would advise to anyone is do let let your parrot be unsupervised as they will chew up everything.  And yes, if you rearrange the furniture they get a little disoriented.  He will eat anything that he sees me eat and actually prefers people food to seeds.  Lucky for him, I eat a lot of veggies.  I have two other parrots, a mealy Amazon and a red wing, all talk and keep each other company.  They are fun and wonderful companions but do require a lot of care.  There are times I wish I had ear plugs because when they start their screeching it can be over bearing, especially when company comes and we try to have a conversation, they want to be part of it too!  Parrots are not for everyone and people need to realize that they require lots of cleaning, and time, not to mention the cost of food.  They make lots of noise and can be destructive if unsupervised.  I have had birds since I was 18 yrs old, started with Cocktails and progressed to bigger Parrots.  I am old now and do worry about what I will do if something happens to me.

text_content:
I have a 13 year old male CAG. He has been “my” bird up until just recently.  He’s now taken a shine to my daughter.  😊 which is fine with me. Because even though I was his person, he’d bite me all the time. And, I’m not talking love bites. He bit for blood. I have so many scars!  

These birds are so smart! They really shouldn’t be pets.  They belong out in the wild to be free to fly and be birds.  I also have a Senegal parrot.  He’s a very clingy bird that just wants to be with me all the time. He’s smart as well.  But, I think all parrots are a little psycho, they aren’t completely domesticated.  They are still wild and deserve to be free. Again, they deserve to be free to fly.  I think that’s why people give up parrots. They don’t realize the work that parrots are and they don’t want to deal with them.  Or, sadder still, they keep them in dark rooms, feed them and forget them.  

No, mine will never be let free.  I’ll make sure they are taken care of, even after I’m gone.  If you feel you must get a parrot, please, please adopt a bird.  Please do not purchase one.  If people adopt, it would send a message to the bird breeders and the stores.

text_content: Excellent advice. We've had six Amazons (two napes, three blue fronts, one double yellow head), most of them for decades (we both did rescue work for many years, which is how they acquired us), and while they're quite different than greys, there are a lot of overlaps. With most parrots, it's much better to have at least two so they can keep each other company when you're gone. (Do not put them in a single cage, even temporarily--that can lead to disaster.) Also, they need a lot of time out of their cages--at least four hours a day. (We leave ours out every waking hour except when we're gone.) And they're incredibly destructive, with a seemingly special taste for rare books, LPs, CDs, furniture, kitchen cabinets, baseboards, doors, etc., etc. So, if you have parrots, unless you're the type of jerk who'll keep them locked up, be prepared for a lot of destruction: they're greater teachers regarding the unimportance of physical things. Finally, do not clip their wings. They're happier and more confident when they're flighted; you'll need to be vigilant about keeping doors closed (screen and security doors are a good idea), but they'll be happier for it.

text_content: I had one for a little while but it never talked so I traded it with someone who had a double yellow head Amazon (and that bird talked up a storm).  What I will say what I loved about the African Gray is they are not hard biters while the Amazon can deliver a vicious bite.  The other thing is the Gray is a very dusty bird that has this very fine dander they use to keep clean with fewer baths.  While not needing so many baths is good it also means it tends to be quite dusty around the birds cage or perch.  Sadly the Amazon while very friendly with me and other men for some reason it did not like women at all and would actively attack them (including my wife) and when we had kids I was just not willing to risk the bird injuring an infant so it had to go.  I truly miss having the parrot now but always think fondly of the time I had with them in my life.

text_content: I have one CAG that I have wanted for years. I got him when he was 13w-14w old (he just turned 1) he is my first parrot. I had done tons of research over about a 2 year time span of learning everything I could. I decided to add one to my family.  There were a lot of people who frowned not only on me getting a baby vs. rescuing but also choosing a grey as my first bird. It is honestly so sad that bigger birds are shown as a "advanced/intermediate" level no bird is an easy pet. Don't expect everything to always be fun and easy, there are always rough patches. All parrots require a lot of time and engagement but are so worth the effort in the end. Every species has both advantages and disadvantages and no one bird is right for everyone. Parrots live a very long time, it doesn't make sense to get one bird just to help prepare you for another ( the one you have your heart set on) and also is not really fair for that bird. Please always do your research. However, don't let talk turn you away from the bird you want regardless of if you are a first time owner or not. I am so happy with my grey and I would have done the same thing looking back. It breaks my heart to think I could have compromised with smaller "beginner bird" and not have my baby Yoshi today.  Ill end this rant here although I could go on...lol Reflecting, I wish I would have had this perspective when I was deciding to get my first bird.

text_content: I just got my 2 parrots as rescues from the parrot trust Scotland called Cody and coco. In 4 weeks Cody has managed to stop taking haldol medication for the first time in years and coco has opened up to men when before she would only interact with women. I’m so proud of the work I’ve done. They where both caged parrots and didn’t want to come out their cages for anything. Not food motivated so was tough. Now when I go in to their bedroom they literally bang their beaks on the cage to get out and come be where I am. It’s been one of the best experiences of my life and it’s very hard but the reward out weighs any amount of work. They would bite me and cause bleeding and bruising at the start. They really did not want anything to do with anything that wasn’t being alone in their cage. So for anyone worrying about this please don’t. If you are willing to PROVE To them you will care for them no matter what and no matter how much they bite and scratch you you’ll still love them and be kind to them I used to sit and eat dinner in front of their cage or I’d sit watching videos on the laptop on the floor and talk to them even tho they acted like they hated me. Now I’m their absolute everything. Please If u can help them and are willing to work they can open up and love again

text_content: Let me tell you about my parrot, Sammee. At nite Sammee waits for me to sit and recline. She starts chirping from her cage for me. I go get her n recline. She stays on my chest til time for bed. But here's the kicker. She doesn't want to leave. Ill say "step up" and she starts backing up and won't get on. When she can go no further she'll flip on her back.  She doesn't want to leave me. So I'll rub her feet and try again. When I finally get her to step up she'll reach for my pajamas to hang on to me. Sammee speaks about 250 words and makes many sounds but she is the most loving pet I've ever had. I had a cockateil that was so loving too. I spend time with my pets and birds are time consuming. Do not get if you don't have time. They need the love.

text_content: My male eclectus seems to be the exception to a lot of things, it's weird. He's super energetic, especially during trick training time, he never bites, we trained him out of regurgitating on us (he just does it to his toys and shiny things now), and he is really social with everyone who comes over. He never screamed until we rescued a female eclectus who taught him to make the worst alarm call in the history of birds 😂. If you can train an eclectus as a baby, you can pretty much get the eclectus you want. It's very difficult to train rescues, I've found. I wouldn't trade my eclectus for anything, he's our little baby and he knows it 😂

text_content:
My aunt and uncle have a beautiful cockatoo! I desperately wanted one when I was younger, so I spent as much time with Bonita (yeah they don’t get points for name creativity😂❤) as I could!
Until I was put in charge of her for 2weeks when I was a teenager, while my aunt and uncle went on a cruise for their anniversary….
Bonita was chill for about the first 4-5 days (which was previously the longest time her “parents” had ever been gone and left me in charge of her before this cruise)— after that, all heII broke loose.  
She totally stopped eating(yes, she went on a hunger strike), she “screamed” almost nonstop and yelled for “mom/dad” (which was heartbreaking), she started destroying everything around the house that she could—and finally, she started ripping feathers out. 
After about 3 days of pure Bonita anarchy, they had to end their trip early and pay for a flight back home before the cruise was even over—because I simply couldn’t get Bonita to calm down, no matter what I tried. 

Sure as the sun rises, almost immediately after they get home—she starts eating normally, no longer shrieked all day and stopped destroying everything in sight!

I still love cockatoos (and Bonita) but I definitely reevaluated my desire for one after that. 😂❤
That was about 25 years ago and they still have never attempted to take another trip that long again, because they don’t want to risk another Bonita meltdown.

text_content:
As an Aussie, we see a lot of escaped/released pet cockatoos - you can tell  because they're usually solo not in a flock and they're extra social and less fearful of people. We have one that occasionally visits us - it's very pointed about telling us what it likes in our garden! Any time we plant parsley, it's mysteriously ripped out and usually deposited onto our downstairs neighbour's balcony! More than once, we've had to knock on their door and ask if we could our plant back please! 

In a flock, they're one of the funniest birds ever - imagine a small child trying to do acrobatics...whilst eating...and talking... They're upside down, food in one claw, usually screeching up a storm and often trying to shoo another cockatoo away with their wings. Then times that by about 100 and put it in the most publicly visible place. It is a magnificent and hilarious sight to behold!

text_content: I worked at a doggy daycare and my boss had a couple cockatoos.  She was the perfect owner for one tho, well knowledged about all animals, single and lonely as mean as that might sound, and lived in a pet shop so she had the perfect place to store the parrots.    You can’t put into perspective how intimidating they are tho, sure they might act cute and talk but anytime that beak is near you there is a chance you’ll get bit.    I used to have to sweep the floor and the cockatoos would follow me around the whole time If they were out their cage trying to bite my legs.    If they were in the cage anytime I tried to sweep under a cage I had a massive beak sticking between the bars trying to bite me or the broom.     Not to mention the when they are out of the cage it is the loudest screeching you’ll ever hear, just constant parrot squawking at a 10/10 volume level for as long as they want to squawk which can be hours.    I love cockatoos to death but I’d never consider owning one but any chance I ever get to interact with one I’ll take the opportunity.

I'll have you know the best pet dinosaur is Rocco, but cockatoos are certainly up there :)
You did fail to mention that occasionally birbs like to chew on things, like wooden blocks, or literally the wall.
As a little anecdote on what looking after a parrot is like, I wake up to Rocco, when he flies off to bed he flies back if I don't follow. I need to convince him that I am coming back when I go to the washroom. When I open the washroom door he is either standing in front waiting for me, or he flew downstairs looking for me and/or trouble. If I go out he sulks when I get back. When I used to close his cage door (been over a year since I have, although I do close the room door still), he would grab on to it with his feet and flap as hard as he could while screaming. He is rather quiet normally actually, first squawk in weeks was in response to him hearing the birb squawks in this video. I have scars from him disagreeing on things, like me sharing my phone, or a pen I was using, or when bedtime was. Oh, and he is <<MOSTLY>> potty-trained, but he also tries to game the system of him getting a treat for going potty in the right place. Little con artist dinosaur :P

text_content: I have 6 of them, all adopted. There is a reason why people get rid of them. Their snuggling is because they are not socialized the natural way, and they demand this every day. The mess, destruction, biting and the screaming is not for everyone. They are NOT beginner's pets. They are perfect for someone who is home all day, with nothing else to do but to cater to their cockatoo. Do not have neighbors - their decibel level is higher than any other parrot. And they live for a long time. They are also prone to plucking, and they can get prolapse, which is very expensive to treat - and many people (and other parrots) are allergic to the feather dust, which is very fine and gets into everything. Every new cage lasts a few weeks and then it needs new doors , latches or gates.They can pop locks and cage-bars and destroy your woodwork in minutes.

video_title: The Truth Behind Owning A Cockatoo | Galah Cockatoo
video_description: Bogie and I get lots of questions regarding what it's like to own a cockatoo. From screeching loudly to attacking faces, owning a ...
text_content:
This is a great vid. It explains everything a too mama goes thru. Yeah I thought I had my bird skills down, but a cockatoo will make you question that once or twice... at least. Galahs maybe more. Lol. 

Granted I have a very calm and cooperative female. But is a huge part of your day, and like ours get music and dance lessons and such. But cockatoos will make you wonder at times, if you have what it takes as a bird mom, lol. So good job explaining that. All good stuff to consider before... research, research, RESEARCH! That’s what I always say. I spent a decade learning before I adopted mine, but also had been learning my whole life really cuz wanted one as a kid. But I wanted to do it right. And even then you’re still not prepared when it comes to toos... cuz you’ve just adopted a human toddler basically. 

I remember for 10 years I heard a lot in the bird community when I’d inquire about a too (I wanted to take my time and wait for the right one, & that totally worked, I knew her when I saw her. And that’s clearly obvious with you 2 as well). 

But experts would often say “most people aren’t cockatoo people. Only a certain few bird moms.” So I kept learning, but also kept thinking I was probably one of them and it would happen one day. 

Cuz it takes a special type of bird mom. Turns out I was one of those peeps where cockatoo and I just go well together... and now it’s my life (but I love it!), & I’m even doing a study on cockatoo behavior in captivity,.. in a normal home life with cockatoos. To develop a better guide for their emotional and psychological needs. 

I’ve cone to the conclusion they need a totally integrated family life as if they were a toddler. All of the learning, all of the enrichment, etc. Youre clearly one of us too, lol. Cuz now you all of these things too and it’s great you take him to bible study and such. it’s beautiful tbh. Passing down all you’ve learned. Well done on this vid. 

Did we remember to get as one last reason: 

Cockatoos have a way of seeing into your soul, and psyching you out that leaves you feeling rattled and unnerved, like they just powered into your soul and made notes for later... it’s creepy when it happens. Cuz that one is a good one, and how you handle that moment when it occurs might determine the rest of your relationship with that bird. cuz they’re that smart! 

They do this look that conveys both that they just saw into your soul AND that they then might use what they saw against you later, at the same time, and it can be unnerving when you first experience it... I’m sure you’ve dealt with that by now. That look where they just mentally documented something they saw in you that you didn’t see yourself... and are gonna save that for later. Lol. 😂 

I remember going to friends when that first happened, crying “omg I didn’t think she could get to me like that! Like I let her get into my head... Holy crap!” 😂😂😂😂😂🙈 “and now Idk what she might be planning!” Thank goodness other cockatoo moms quickly told me, “you’re ok, you got this, you finally got the Soul Stare & now you’re feeling really inadequate as a bird mom. We’ve all been there.” Like they knew cuz they’d been thru it too. Lol. So having a cockatoo friends support system when you first adopt is also a good idea. So I’m adding that. You’ll have questions. 

Now I know what do in those situations, and it rarely occurs cuz she’s given up trying. I just do it right back & then she gets unnerved, lol... she did it twice in the 1st year... had me questioning all my skills as a bird mom. 😂😂😂🙈 

My girl also loves Dance Moms & I give her dance lessons... she has goals in life. I’m like “at least she has that psych-out stare down, cuz every dance kid learns that for competition and this little too is a master at it!” And then she realized when JoJo Siwa did the exact same psych-out stare on DWTS this week and cheered. 😂🤷🏼‍♀️ All of Abby’s kids learn that for competition. I just hate when she does it to me. Lol. 

So we do a lot of toddler type enrichment. Right now the birds are happily raising their own interactive cyber pet (toy) & learning ABC’s & the arts. We get kids toys for them along with their birdie chew toys. They’re learning to play a tiny piano and everything. The more we do like that the more I watch their personalities grow and things go smoothly with momsplaing why we have certain rules and stuff.

text_content: Sharing your life with a Cockatoo is a lifestyle. Their demands will take over your life. I had 10 years experience working with parrots, including working in an aviary before deciding to adopt a Mollucan. We did our research including visiting bird sanctuaries and even the National zoo. I still had no idea what we were in for when we brought him home. As the video states they have the intelligence of a human toddler and share many of the same traits. They throw tantrums, they throw food, they bite, and their scream has been measured at 110db  (compared to 130db for a jet engine at 100 feet). He's been our faithful companion for over 38 years, but I know I speak for both of us when I say I there are days we wish we didn't have him.

I got my due to last owner couldn’t tame her and she sent one of them to the hospital. I ended up taming her, but ended up buying the bird from them. I dealt and train 100’s of parrots. My all time favorite was Baby my my umbrella cockatoo and reason I bought her. This bird was one of the most aggressive and dangerous bird I come across as she was caged up for years due to no one wanted to handle her much and she was scared of pretty much everything. So she would bite at any chance she could get. She turn out to be my best feather companion and goes where I go, even to work with me. She rides on my shoulder at stores and etc. she now we’ll socialize and people even play and talk to her. She doesn’t no longer show any sign of past aggression. One of the reason cockatoos are my favorite is the fact they are temperamental. 

It is the inexperienced owners that are at fault. Theses birds get a bad name like pit bulls do. They are super smart and love being around people in general and can be very gentle. They only scream out of bad habits, due to want attention and they learned that how they get it. It easy to train them out of it.

video_description: shorts #parrot #cockatoo #australia #parrotvideo #animal #animals #animalfacts #animalshorts #animaldocumentary #nature ...
text_content: I started watching parrot videos and thought about adopting one but decided to wait and do more research and think about it some more because I knew they were a major commitment (plus I thought maybe I should get a smaller one like a Conure instead).  After several months I went to a bird rescue sanctuary in Fargo, ND and was just going to look, but when I glanced into the big bird (no Sesame Street pun intended) room an umbrella cockatoo stretched out her neck to look at me so I went over to her a little nervous and the worker told me she’s friendly and to hold out my hand and say step up which I did.  The bird climbed onto my hand and I lowered my arm.  When I did that the bird named Bella laid her head on my chest and I fell in love.  While she has destroyed some good shoes and trim on the walls, I wouldn’t change a thing because I couldn’t imagine life without her.  I’ve had her six years now and don’t regret anything.

text_content:
I was looking for a celebes.
Then I found female galah with biting issues. "That pink light version of a cocatoo can't be that bad" I tought 😂 Little did I know 🤣🤣🤣 
She took me as her personal bestie and servant, at first we had really nice time. 10 months later I was secretly thinking that maybe it would be the best for all to put her down before she makes some serious injuries to someone. As my last chance I decided to take a risk and got her a birdfriend. I got male baby galah. Female stopped biting hoomans in few days. They are not friends but now the female is friendly to my and my family. Male bites to my son but it is more playing than real violence. Male allso owns me now and if I want to spend some time with the female, we must go to another room 😅
I don't know are they happy, I hope so. I'm happy, I have them both alive and well. And the chaos and mayhem and noise that they make, my horrible soulbirds.
BUT I HOPE NO-ONE GETS A GALAH THINKING THAT THE PINK GUY IS SOME NICE AND CALM COCATOO LIGHT VERSION!!! It is a beast, dangerous one, an inferno with  wings, stay away of them!!! 🤣🤣🤣🤣
Don't get me wrong, Galah is the best bird to someone who is mad enough to love them. They just are....well...galahs 😍

text_content: I've been around moluccan  cockatoos for upwards of 30 years. The one thing I noticed is you cannot be frightened of them, they will take advantage of that. At present I have six of them. Two females, Dolly and Pumpkin, that would never even consider biting me. One other female, April, gives me a pinch occasionally but it's only if I'm not paying enough attention. I have one small male, Marin, that used to be a bit aggressive but now that he's approaching 30 years old he's settled down. Two other 15-year-old males, Louie and huckleberry, we're passed around to different homes over the years because they were a bit aggressive but now that they've become part of our family they are just the most wonderful sweethearts.  Cockatoos are awesome. Oh, and it will be a much happier experience if you're handy at finish carpentry.

text_content: We have one we adopted , 22 year old male.   And we discovered he has a toll, you can pay in time spent around him,  or in blood.  The more blood you spill by holding him,  the faster he will come around to be your friend.   I'm his favorite and basically as since day 1 which was VERY odd,  he attached right to me I must look something like his former owner.  But randomly he would still just bite me as if he realized I was not who he thought.   But within a year we had a good relationship.   My wife and or any woman he seems to really REALLY hate, and has even jumped off his cage to attack my mother and mother in law.   As for my wife, she reduced the time cost by adding a little blood to it and they are also friends now. And it helps a lot to read the bird,   I made games out of everything he did, when he would get upset and start making high pitched scream sounds, I would lightly tap him on his back and make a " bock bock bock " sound,  and at some point that became a game, and he quit doing it.   Also Bobble Bobble, Kirby Coaster (flip him upside down and back) and others.   His screams we were able to replace with him barking.    We learned he hated the spray bottle we would use to mist him,  so we used it as threat not a bath and he would quit screming, but he replaced screaming himself with barking like our dogs.   So now he barks at things he don't like, and we are okay with that since its MUCH lower volume lol.

text_content:
As a parrot owner of 27 years - THANK YOU for giving the negative points to owning a parrot.  Everyone shows the cutesy, cuddly parts of life with a parrot, but rarely do they give the warnings.  I studied them for a year before I decided on a Congo African Grey.  I knew I would get bitten.  I knew they required time and attention.  I have many scars on my fingers, and one or two on my lips.  We love our boys, but we also understand them.  You HAVE to be able to read body language.  Greys are not as attention seeking a 'toos are, but they can be very aggressive during mating season - spring & fall.  They do not allow deviation from a set schedule, and they need a schedule.
Our orange-winged Amazon is very sweet and gentle.  The only time he bites is when there is a child in the house.  When a child enters, he no longer wants much to do with me.  He wants to cuddle with the child.  Otherwise he is patient, he is sweet - but when he screams - he makes your eardrums shake.  And he screams fairly often.
I really appreciate you putting it out there so people know what they are getting into.

text_content: I have two sun conures, a yellow naped amazon,  and a male umbrella cockatoo. Figured if I could handle sun conure noise, I'd be find with a cockatoo. Man, was I wrong! Basically, it's like living with a 3yr old who's bi-polar . My other birds aren't nearly as needy. Thought I did my due diligence before getting him, but there's really not many people willing to tell the truth.  Everything you said was dead on, down to having to use locks on his cage. Took two locks (the one with keys) to seal the nesting box entrance because he broke the bolts holding it together, and his cage is 60 x 60!  Honestly, they shouldn't be pets. They're not domesticated like dogs and cats. Love my little guy to death, and I have no intention of placing him in yet another home.......but if I knew what I was really in for before getting him, I wouldn't have done it..... that's the truth. Having said that, I've never laughed so hard at a birds antics......but that's maybe 5% of the time. The rest is not always so easy. I'd love to know how you got him not to scream so much. My guy is good as long as he can see me. Can't even go to the bathroom without him screaming the house down. Good thing we live on 40 acres on top of a mountain. 😂

text_content: I have one and i regret getting one everyday, my family decided to get African gray so we went to a pet shop and bought a new born African gray, the bird turned out to be disabled and it had heart problems so the shop gave us our money back. They offered us a baby Umbrella cockatoo instead and we took it. The cockatoo was a female and she was so big compared to regular cockatoos. We didn’t know how bad cockatoos were before we bought them we just had conures before. My pet cockatoo is 7 now and she just has so many issues. She gets super sexual with us so we cant touch her below the neck, we learned that the hard way after she laid 4 eggs between the ages of 4-6💀 terrible ik. We trained her to stay on her cage with our other bird which is a nanday conure. They get alone just fine and rarely fight but when they do she always does something horrible like once she bite a hole in the other birds beak. She has never not once bit a human before though. She so loving and sweet but also a demon at the same time. She has gotten out and ruined tv remotes and cords before. She also has butt problems because she prolapse herself whenever she feels like she doesn’t have enough attention. We had to result into spraying her with water because she pushes her organ out if she doesnt get sprayed. She has been doing her butt thing for years now and she never cleans her butt because she started at a young age. Not her fault at all by the way she has behavior issues because we gave her too much attention. I was like 10 when we got her and I’m 17 now. Lover her to death but she would be better off in the wild 😂. She’s not aggressive at all and she is well trained, if we say go up she runs up her cage, if we say get in the cage she runs right in.  We fixed her sexual issue by not letting her on the couch with us. She has to stay on the cage, once in a whole we’ll let her sit on our knee on the couch but other then that she stays on her cage. She chose my father from day one because he never feared her at all and he is also the person she fears the most. She loves my dad but also fears my dad so much, we don’t abuse her at all btw she just fears my dad because he has a big beard and a deep voice and he’s very tall. She has eacaped the cage so many times but recently she has been escaping her cage and staying on top of her cage with the other bird which is progress 😂. She’s just starting to become better because we try to not cuddle her or anything and she talks sooooo much. She got her butt problem original from a stomach illness btw, it started from that and we gave her attention when she would push her stuff out, now she thinks that’s like the bell that get’s everyone’s attention. She is a handful and i regret wanting her lol. (Edit) btw i just wanted to mention she has a huge cage with a nice special structure on top where she chills for most of the day and it’s in the living room so she sees us all day and she gets attention all the time.

text_content: As a carer for a cockatoo I found your video so true,  one thing i will mention that I have found especially with my Greater crested sulphur cockatoo called Buster but also with other species of cockatoos that I have cared for, is how he will pretend to like a visitor,  No matter how often I warn my visitors not to trust his sweet ways, He will act so sweet and charming just to lure them closer so he can bite them, as you know his bite can be nasty.  I keep him caged when I have Visitors but he will act so nice and loving that he has bitten five visitors.  He is so intelligent  you can see his thought process but Too's can move there head so fast from a head bowed down to bite the finger is so fast and quicker than a humans reaction.  I have never been bit by a Cockatoo and I can do anything with Buster but he is a one person bird.  I have over 50 birds in my home and most flying free,  His cage was for a green winged macaw but the strength in his beak has bent some of the bars and removed one,  How ever He gets on with the other flock members providing they are not cockatoos or eclectus parrots and most nights two of my budgies and a pair of zebra finches will get through the bars to sleep next to him, also three of my ringnecks and two of my amazons will sleep on top of his cage at night.   Thank you for making this video and for the love and understanding of your birds,

text_content:
Thank you for doing this. Educating people about not impulse buying is so important because so many kids can watch things and get this wistful image of the perfect pet in a perfect world.

I know I have a personal dream to one day be able to live a relatively good life that's perfect for raising a Mollucan Cockatoo. I've done a lot of research and I know full well what it takes to take care of even the simplest of parrots - and trust me. There is no simple parrot.

You have to devote a lot of time, money, and love into them. Most, if not all, parrots are as intelligent as a 3 year old child. A toddler! And they live upwards of 20 to 80 years on average, depending on size and species. They're lifetime commitments. You'll literally be raising a toddler for a majority of your living life. While that can be rewarding for those who know what they're up against and are prepared for that, then they're perfect additions to the family. But you should never impulse buy any animal. Ever. You should always do your research and prepare, be it a mouse, a dog, a parrot, or a horse. Always make sure you can actually PROVIDE before you OWN.

text_content:
Dear community, 
as a birdowner for more than twenty years I can confirm, that everything told in the video is absolutely right!
 I just want to add, if you get a parrot, 
PLEASE give him a bird partner from the same species! 
No one can groom feathers so wonderful as another bird can! 
For all parakeets a partner of the same species is very important! 
In my country (germany) it is even forbidden to keep parrots individually.

Few people can spend as much time on their birds as Nimal and Claire, not every parrot has such a good life like Mikey and Mia.

Normally, then, such a smart and social being sits alone and unhappy at home for hours, usually even locked up.
To expect something like this from your bird is not love, it is just "wanting to have", and these magical creatures do not deserve such a sad life!

Mikeys life has been wonderful so far, but I'm sure his beautiful Mia is the icing on the cake of his life! 🐦🐦💞

text_content:
Im an animal behaviourist who specialises in macaws, and I have to say that for 18 months, an unresearched impulse buy, you are doing great! As to all the things he does that you dont love very much (carpet removal, door pruning, screeching, grabbing, etc) these are things that are easily fixed with help from a parrot behaviourist, in person or via live video on pm, etc. Lessons that you, your partner and Mikey all get to learn together, problem solving where you all have a piece of the puzzle.
I would recommend you finding a macaw behaviorist sooner rather than later, to help you fix the negative issues. MY blue and gold wears a harness and goes everywhere I do, as he is a heart monitor and pre warns before I'm going to collapse, so believe me when I say your Mikey is SMART, and being a little rotter pushing the boundries with you, playing at "dumb birdy" ...and conned you successfully! Welcome to parronthood 😁 (some days your fid is just going to be a d@#&) best thing you can do atm is learn his real capabilities re understanding you both, and what his iq and eq ratings are, then tailor his learning appropriately.
I am worried about your mesh by the way. It doesnt look stainless steel and anything else is poisonous to macaws in particular... It is why most bird rooms have only free standing perches and those hung from the ceiling (hook screwed through a frisbee so it cant be chewed out of ceiling) and all lights and windows covered with perspex or stainless cages, and a security screen door in place of the bedroom door!
Same with most bird toys you buy... licking and chewing at chrome chains kill macaws.

text_content:
I wish it was required to take lessons about the bird you want to adopt or buy before you are allowed to own one. People should learn about the needs of their future companion and also get exposed to the noise and judge if they can handle it. And it should be included in those lessons what a naked bird looks like. One that has plucked itself to the point where it can't grow its feathers back and has to stay naked for the rest of his considerably long life. 
I've been living with one or more pets for almost all my life which is nearly 60 years now. And I got all my animals first and started to read books upon books afterwards. You'd think that I got wise after it happened once or twice, but it happened over and over. The reason is simple. There was a pet in need that the owner wanted to get rid of and I felt I had the space, the time and the level of commitment to take it in. 
I'm glad it never was a parrot because I'm not sure I could have handled that without being the one being unhappy in the relationship. Because once I commit I stick with it especially if the pet bonded with me and relied on me to take proper care. I like birds and I enjoyed living with a "flock" of 6 budgies that spent most of the day on a bird tree in the living room outside their cage. But that's worlds apart from living with a parrot. So I'm gonna stick watching parrots on YouTube enjoy other people loving their birds.

text_content:
One correction: Macaws live around 30 years in the wild, but can live up to 100 years in captivity. They are wild animals and as such are very difficult pets to care for, and only an experienced exotic bird owner should own one.

They’re incredibly intelligent, friendly, and loyal, but require as much attention and work as a small child, AND they can literally deafen you with their incredibly loud screams, _AND_ are capable of biting through a broomstick with their beaks.

I don’t say this to be mean to macaws, but because I rescue parrots, and strongly believe you ought to require a license to own certain larger species which are sold through the exotic pet trade. Macaws and cockatoos often end up having at least two, three, even four plus owners in their lifetimes because people think it will be like owning a dog, but can’t handle the noise or the biting or the constant care needed, and the poor macaws end up homeless in a pet rescue facility.

*Please do NOT buy a macaw. If you are committed to owning one, ALWAYS rescue a homeless bird instead of buying one!*

text_content:
We just rescued a blue and gold Macaw named Roscoe. We were told he we *roughly* 14 years old, but the people we got him from said they only had him for four years, so they're aren't even sure. 

He had quite an attachment to me very quick, which is crazy because I've never been a bird owner before until I met my Fiancée, who hand raised and sold birds as a teenager and has dealt with them her whole life. 

But he is very territorial and will bark and scream at us if we get too close or hug and kiss on one another. And if I'm not in the room with her, he will snap at her and try to bite, but never does that to me. She hasn't done anything to him, and I haven't done anything special to deserve his undying love and affection. So i was curious if that seems to be typical of them. 

For some experience context for myself; She had a cockatiel when we got together, named Blaiz, and we rescued a blind and arthritic orange winged amazon parrot shortly after we got together, named Peter. Both alive and as well as can be. 
Blaiz is now 15, cranky, old boy, but he belonged to her son, who unfortunately passed away a few months back, so there is 0 chance we get rid of him. 

Peter is 43. He was in a home that didn't care about him. His original owner passed away a few years back from old age, and the lady we got him from didn't want him, nor did she take care of him. His beak was so overgrown that it's now permanently shifted his jaw so his mouth is crooked. And his talons were so overgrown that he couldn't walk on his feet, to which one grew very stiff, and now he can't open it all the way. (Vet said there was nothing we could do about it because it had progressed too far for medicine to be of any use.)

Roscoe is very healthy, aside from the awful diet the last couple had him on, which consisted of peanuts, lots of fried potatoes, and paper. 🙃

We have fixed the diet. He now has a regular diet of fresh fruits and veggies as he should, with a nutriberri blend that we mix plumage food into. And the occasional peanut or pistachio for a treat. 

He's a tricky little guy, but he is a huge sweetheart. He has an attitude, but he is much larger than Peter, so while he has never attacked me, (he snapped at me once, but didn't hurt or even leave a mark, so I'm sure he was just playing) I'm still pretty nervous around him. He let's me pick him up, and pet his head. I haven't tried his face around his eyes yet, so I'm going to try that today and see if he's okay with it. 

I was just wondering if you had any tips for helping him adjust to her? She's always been a huge bird lover, and she was so excited to get Roscoe. He was not very nice to her the first week we had him, and she got very frustrated by him, and actually yelled (her yells aren't very yelly) at him. Blaiz is actually a total asshole because her son didn't really take care of him, and I've seen Blaiz bite her and draw blood and she's never gotten mad enough to yell at him. So I can tell that she's really upset by his behavior, more so because he is so nice to me. I just want to do my best to help him adjust to her, too. 

I'm sorry that was a lot of stuff to say. 😅

text_content: Learning your bird's boundaries is a huge part of things. I think many birds end up in rescue situations because of this. People don't understand that it takes time to learn how to read them and when a bird bites they blame it on the bird being "aggressive" rather than their own lack of patience and understanding. text_content: I work behavioral issue macaws to help them become adoption candidates. This is very true. Most bites I’ve gotten have come from fear. I’ve worked with macaws who have been through insane amounts of abuse, I’ve worked with birds that would bite everyone and everything, yet they never hurt me to the point of needing stitches or permanent disfigurement. I’ve gotten some nasty bites here and there, I’ve collected a few scars even, but it’s a far cry from the real damage these guys can do. They are pretty gentle by nature. I love seeing how they transform and learn better boundaries as well. I learn  how to keep that bird from feeling the need to bite. Birds will bite but most of the time they give very clear warnings before hand. It definitely is about knowing your bird as well, birds can bite not just because they’re scared but also because they are over stimulated. Be gentle, patient, and kind with a biting bird. Most will learn boundaries over time or at least you will learn how to pick up on the warning signs.

text_content:
I will say this as an umbrella cockatoo dad of five years:

My bird (Einstein) is one of the most affectionate and emotionally intelligent, if not needy, animals I’ve ever owned. He explores with his beak and having a safe and fruitful relationship with him has been all about learning how to read his body language, understand his precursors to naughty or stress behavior, and setting/enforcing boundaries when needed. 

However, with that said…. He has bitten me and my sister very badly quite a few times. Being a bird owner is about knowing that yes, you can expect nips, but you need to be prepared for the occasional major bite and approach it with the understanding that going forward…. Learn from WHY you got bit. 

For every single major bite I’ve sustained from Einstein, I can 100% say that it was my fault for not listening to his body language. You have to know your bird inside and out, but to a degree I disagree that you shouldn’t expect proper bites. I find that to be unrealistic 

Love your birds by the way, it’s obvious that you take incredible care of them and know their body languages so so so well. You’re a great bird dad

text_content: i rescued a parrot.. she had burned wings and broken feet .. she is scared of ppl.. i thought she will recover after some time.. but she is still scared of me.. she tries to fly.. without wings.. i dont want to keep her caged.. i leave her cage door open and she sits behind cage hidden.. she tried to hide under bed and tables .  but i was determined to not let her hide this way.. so i tried to go near till we agreed that i wont scare her if she remained on her cage.. i also tried to accommodate her n gv her doll house and a carriage but she was just happy sitting on cage.. so i hv placed seeds inside cage so when she goes in.. i close the cage and bathe her once a week..sometimes twice.. she absolutely loves bathing n she isnt scared when i bathe her.. but she rarely visits her cage .. if she goes n sees me coming she comes outside immediately 😂.. she thinks ill cage her .. i guess.. i keep fruits vegetables near her cage outside on table.. she comes and eat.. she only ate apple, cucumbers very little, she ignored almonds, walnuts , pumpkin seeds, carrots.. majorly she only eats sunflower seeds.. i tried showing her parrot videos so she doesn't go in depression .. she tried to touch screen n touch parrots 😢😢she is lonely but doesn't want humans.. she ones tried to jump from balcony and got stuck in net..then she cut the net n came back.. i was there but didnt dare to go near as she is scared n i was worried she will hurt herself more😢 please advise

text_content:
thank you for this video
I love the look of these birds, how curious they are and so on and so forth

but I nearly jumped the gun
a lady was trying to give away her 2 year old Hyacinth Macaw to me ....
I live in a small apartment, and work night shifts, I do have a bird of my own (Indian Ringneck Parrot) but he's been adopted as well .... he hasn't been treated too well by his previous owners, so now i am living with consequences, and he hates fingers, my phone and anything to do with them, bites painfully through the shirt and you may even bleed underneath, but loves attention and wants affection too (just not from fingers or hands) ...
I am glad I saw your video before taking on such a huge commitment and responsibility, that I don't have a space for nor the time for ...

taking in small parrots is fine-ish
but I should never think of getting a parrot unless I have a private house with a garden I guess.
thank you for everything. Even with all the intense care and behaviour I still want a Macaw, but can't do anything about it now
I'd feel awful keeping such a large bird in a cage  when I can't be around due to work.

text_content: Great vid. We have a Green Wing and yep he was a shoulder rusher too....trained him and now he doesn't even feel comfortable up there after 3yrs.....he likes to stay on forearms....still working to get him to be on your hand with thumb over one foot....some success there, always a work in progress. Anyway, lots of people think they want the big birds......maybe you could do videos on how hard they really are. Like how much they eat, how quickly they destroy $30+ toys, how needy they get when bored, how noisy they can be when bored or even just crazy noisy they are when happy. Don't forget the messes they make...and don't forget big bird equals big poop and lots of it. Also how about how smart, mischievous, and manipulating they can really be. When our GW is bored and wants to play with the other birds like our cockatiel, he will go let him out of his cage. How about how quirky they can be as individuals, if we don't change out one of our GW stands poop papers down below it, he will pull the papers off himself and throw them around. Im sure big bird owners have lots of fun(?)quirky bird stories. All that said he is a member of the family and we couldn't be without him......but some days he pushes that pretty hard....🤦

text_content:
I have a very large beautiful male yellow nape  named Cricket.  I have had him for 27 years (he is 28) and I got him from a sanctuary where he'd been placed after being abused.  I think HR is the perfect parrot, but it took a lot of time and work to gain his trust, and he still barely tolerates men.  

He is relatively quiet, and sing and talks and doesn't scream except rarely and only if he senses danger like a hawk or helicopter or cat.and is incredibly loving in his way and extremely affectionate.  I am disabled an have several chronic illnesses including brittle diabetes, and he learned very early to alert me to low blood sugar emergencies by rubbing vigorously on my cheek and saying "it's OK!"  "You alright?" and not stopping until I ate or drank (of course he got jackpot treats!). If it was late or bedtime he would often not go  in his cage, but run to the bedroom and station himself on the headboard of the bed and would want to watch me all night. Getting him in his cage was hard on those nights!   I live in NYC and he is a fixture in the neighborhood,  If he isn't seen for a  few days during good weather,  people come and ask about him.  He constantly flirts with the neighborhood women calling them to. "come see the birdie", and telling them that they're beautiful. His only flaw is that he's messy,but I wouldn't change a thing about him.  He is a true blessing in my life,  and he also brings smiles to almost everyone who sees him!

He is not a good talker and can be pretty grumpy, and lucky for me, he is on the quiet side.  I was able to live in a lot of apartments with him with no complaints from neighbors….but that isn’t the norm.  My wish is that all people thinking of getting a parrot do their research and make sure they know what they are getting into:  all the healthy food prep, the mess, the noise, the destruction, exotic vet bills, and all the attention they need to keep them happy and well adjusted.  It’s a major commitment, and obviously a lot of people buying parrots do not consider it, or we wouldn’t see such a heartbreaking number of parrots in need of new homes.  Please PLEASE remember these are intelligent, loving creatures who bond very strongly with their owners, it’s not fair to them to not be given the care they deserve, or to be bounced from person to person.

If you think you want a parrot be aware that they live a very long time and also need many hours of attention each day. Do your research and give a new home to a bird from a rescue centre. Do not get a bird if you are out at work all day, they do not deserve to be shut in a cage on their own. Give them love and understanding and they will return it, but by their very nature they will be getting up to mischief and needing constant attention.
A cage should not be a prison but a sanctuary that your bird can use to sleep and feed in.

text_content:

I felt like I finally gained their trust until he nipped my finger then jumped back to the cage, really cheeky.

video_title: Peach Fronted Conure, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
I’ve had mine for 22 years.  He is a doll but not cuddly, I cannot skritch his head.  He is super smart, a pretty good talker.  Very polite and not that messy for a parrot.  His only loud moments are his alarm call when someone is in the yard or I walk past him with a broom, yardstick, etc.  All conures are “nippy” AKA bitey….lol.  My Peach Fronted only bites under extreme duress.  He has really never bitten me.  When I got him, I asked him “can I call you Swee’Pea” and he said “ok”❤❤   He loves to hang out on my shoulder, he laughs and flirts.  
He loves music and has his own playlist he dances to.  
I think they are awesome little birds.

video_title: Peach Fronted Conure, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
Oh, Momo is very a much a nipper, but conures overall can get kinda bitey. That being said, when you know how to read em, you can usually avoid most bites. She's cuddly, but very much on her own terms. Sometimes she's into head scritches, and sometimes she'd just rather sit up next to your arm or something and preen. We keep her in our living room, that way she has access to both a window, TV, and that's usually where atleast one of us is hanging out within eyesight. She has a habit of climbing down from her cage, less now as she's gotten older. Usually happens when someone is not in the room and she gets bored from her toys and stuff. Typically she'll make her way to the main table and climb to where I sit usually. 

She's pretty quiet, but has flocked called for people once in a while. Usually she's her loudest (and kinda scariest) is when she spots like a cat, hawk, or person (Pest control, etc) outside. That, or when you talk on the phone and she tries to figure out what your doing. Never really got her to talk, but again she isn't super loud all the time. I do sometimes get her to make kissing noises! Plus, you can teach em other tricks (I've gotten her to spin!).

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content: So what happens if you never touched your bird inappropriately? I brought home a 7 year old, male Quaker Parrot a year ago. From day one he feigned regurgitation for my fingers and tried to mate with my hands.  I hadn't ever scratched him yet on the head let alone anywhere else. I had researched parrots before getting one so I knew not to rub under the wing and such.  I had only ever had him on my shoulder and perched on my finger. I met him once before to see how he liked me. I spent 3 hours with him before coming back a week later to pick him up.  The first time i met him he flew onto my shoulder, stared me down, gave a head bop and proceeded to rub his beak and face into my beard. I thought he was just friendly but now I suspect he was courting me.  Since bringing him home he frequently gets "excited", even if I just talk to him and even just a short amount of head scratches. I had tried various diets, extra sleep. Attempt to discourage the behavior. I never let him mate with my hand and I will pull away, set him down or put him in the cage but nothing has ever worked.

video_title: Mexican Red Crowned Amazon, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Red Headed Amazon, aka Green Cheeked Amazon, is an insanely fun big personality parrot but the question is, is this the ...
text_content:
What a lovely, insightful video :)

My grandmother got an Amazon back in the eighties and as you might imagine, back then, no one knew how to take care of such a bird. What little information there was, mostly was aimed to make the owners life easier, not ensure the parrot is kept properly. So, after being caught in the wild as a baby, shipped around the world, he spent his first decade in a way too tiny cage, mostly being ignored, because he wasn't handleable and became more and more aggressive over time.
As soon as I was old enough to realize, I tried and succeeded to bond with him. But being a kid still, I too didn't fully understand how to make his life better. Over time, I learned and tried my best to improve his situation, while he still lived with my granny. One day I finally managed to take him with me.
He now has a huge enclosure and since I work from home, he is with me most of the time. I am glad to be able to keep him near me all the time, since finding a bird friend for him was deemed impossible by several experts - he is absolutely terrified of other birds, probably due to what happened during the transport. Alas, he is enjoying attention and love as much as appropriate food. While I try my very best to make his birdy life an awesome one, we still have to work through a lot of issues due to all the years of neglect. But he is doing a great job and is a wonderful friend and I see that he is happy now.
All that being said, I still feel guilty for not helping him sooner, but I will continue to make it up to him.

Seeing you and your peers doing your best to educate people about those animals is just wonderful and I am sure you are saving many of them from ending up in awful situations. Thank you!

text_content: I have one for 10 years so far.  He is feisty, even though we had him as a baby bird.  I’m the only one that can handle him.  He hates my uniform- he will try to bite hard if I put my hand in his large flight cage wearing my uniform.  He loves listening and copying my hubby, but will bite him if he had the chance.   He calls out my kids names all day if he hasn’t seen them.  He copies a few bird calls, crow, seagull mainly.  He loves to listen to other bird chatters.  Wild parrots have landed on his cage.  He goes on a screeching rampage every morning and evening.  We give him walnuts to try to quiet him at those times.  He bows his head for scratches, he fluffs his feathers when asked to shake, open his wings on command, flaps his wings holding onto perch when I say let’s fly.  He also says rawr, I’m a lion. text_content: Many times I've been bothered by the fact that other friends (and even people) have larger, "more difficult" parrots they keep just fine, without any experience  and tell me they're easy, while I have 1 lovebird and find him challenging.  That doesn't match up. Later on I realized parrots aren't difficult  to own. They're difficult to DO RIGHT and just "owning one" doesn't make a success story. It's be easy for anyone to just own a bird - clean it, feed it... if they don't work with it. My friends African grey is scared of everything so she doesn't do much with him. But an inactive, fearful parrot is not a success even if it doesn't cause issues. So it's important  to note that, yes many owners "cope" but their birds intelligence is unused and unsatisfied. I find my lovebird challenging because I try to keep it mentally stimulated, well-trained, which he is. I can do most things with him my friends can't do with their own parrots.

text_content:
Hey there Blue Plant Pets! I just wanted to ask for some help with my bird, since you know a lot about many of them.
I’ve had a few parakeets over the past four years- three total, but sadly two of them died, the last almost a year ago. The parakeet that I have right now is a female yellow-headed, turquoise parakeet, but that isn’t the main problem.
I first got Ari (the parakeet I have right now) along with one other, older parakeet when I was very young. I wasn’t properly trained for these birds, but I at least knew how to feed and water them, and clean their cage. The only thing I wasn’t taught was how to actually take care of them.
The older parakeet had another owner, so she was used to humans. But she died before she could make an impact on Ari. I didn’t properly get her used to me. And although I’ve learned a lot more about taking care of them over the next few years, all the videos or questions or searches have been about first getting birds, not having them for a while.
So, this is the problem I hope you could help me fix. Ari does not let me touch her, let me hold her, or perch on my finger. She most she’ll let me do is hold my finger out for her to gently touch her beak.
Ari has been my friend for years, and I just want to make sure that eventually I’ll be able to show her that I won’t hurt her.

video_title: Eclectus parrots: single bird or a pair? #parrots #eclectus
text_content:
Had an eclectus hen for over 10 years, wish I could afford another one
Ekkes do not like sharing stuff at all, mine was ok sharing food with my conure but would drag my hand out of her bowl if I was messing with it
Most cases my conure would harass her while nesting in my bed to the point she would kick him out of the covers
From experience with over 40+ birds, ekkes are one of the best birds, they like to socialise a lot but get bored with everything easily and really like their food
They are also one of the top smartest parrots, less verbal than an African grey but way more observant

text_content:
I have a "pair" Frank is about 3, Ruby  is a few months younger. Multi bird housegold, 8 'keets,  😅3"tiels and 4 Indian Ringnecks. Frank is my pal, hollers when I arrive and leave, l8kes to hang with me but can occationally lash out. No serious bites tho. Ruby is totally bonded to me. Has even tried to feed me.  They both talk under their covers at night. Ruby is content to sit on my shoulder (Yeah, I know) and offer the occasional comment or preen. Frank is always looking for mischief.
As you say, she has NO use for him. She has laid 4 eggs but no intent to breed. I love them dearly, enjoy their beauty but they indeed need more personal attention. Like so many breeds, people buy them without understanding them to the birds' peril. More education and less salesmanship is needed. But hearing all my "birdies" welcoming the Sun each day? A song of bliss.
Oh yeah, my cats give them a wide berth. Those big ol' beaks...

text_content:
For beginners I totally understand this list. For anyone with parrot experience, it won’t be that bad. I went from a Green Cheek Conure to a Congo African Grey and our African Grey is honestly easier aside from requiring more toys & general stimulation. She’s never bitten anyone hard enough to break skin, and likes to just hang out most of the time. She’s holds a grudge, but is generally very sweet and loving, even to strangers. 

My conure on the other hand used to attack, constantly. He’d fly for peoples faces and literally rip / bite through skin for seemingly no reason. I’ve honestly had better luck with big birds for temperament than the smaller birds. Though cockatiels are angels in my experience.

text_content: I have a rescue red sided eclectus that spent a couple of years (before me) locked in a cage in a closet. I've had Pepper about 2 years now, and he's fully feathered again, stopped his toe tapping and is finally relaxed and happy. After I got him, it took him 2-3 weeks to stop biting me just for the hell of it. It took him about 18 months to actually enjoy being touched and cuddled. He's learned to speak a few more things than when he first arrived, but talking is definitely something that is on his terms. A lot of people don't understand that these birds do EVERYTHING on their own terms and at their own pace. Rescuing an eclectus (or any parrot) is one of the most rewarding things, but OMG does it require patience and persistence. My son rescued a 35 year old triton cockatoo. And i absolutely agree with you here. None of these are beginner birds or for the impatient, but they are amazing birds. But really, nothing beats the morning singing of a sweet little cockatiel. I will always have major love for those guys. I would recommend them every time.

text_content: Budgies are actually great talkers and are really smart, most people don’t realize this because they get them at pet stores as adults and they were never raised to be socialized with humans. It’s best to get them from a store or a breeder that specializes in birds when they’re about 6 weeks old because this is when they’re old enough to leave the nest but still young enough to bond with you. If you want your bird to talk, you should get a male. I’ve never successfully got a female budgie to talk but the bond will still be there if you just want a buddy. For best results, you should take your budgies out as much as possible. Only keep them in their cage when they sleep and when you go out, they learn to talk by being around you and the more social they are, the longer and happier their lives will be

.

.

text_content:
Dude, bless you for this. It is so heartbreaking how many turtles end up neglected, dying young, or being heartlessly dumped in the wild because people don't know what they've signed up for. Anyone who even THINKS about getting a turtle for their kid in my presence is going to get a speech. These babies produce so much waste and live for SO long, and despite ehat appears to be popular belief, it's not cool to put them in a room by themselves and leave them alone all the time. More people need to upload videos like this one. I get really upset when I see people encouraging strangers on the internet to adopt turtles without getting into the details, especially the lifelong commitment aspect.

On behalf of myself, my ASN Xeno, and all turtles, thank you so much!!!

text_content: Living in the (sort of) north east of the east coast, my friend and I had caught a box turtle when we were around 12 years old. Neither of us knew how to keep a turtle properly, and with this being a "box" turtle... we threw it in a box on her back deck, and checked in on it daily. I know for a fact that we didn't feed it properly, nor did we provide the space and 'amenities' that they might need. Thankfully, one day it was gone and seemed to escape off of her deck. I like to think it's still alive and well, since it's only been ~10-15 years or so.
