video_title: Our little Eastern Box Turtle is so handsome🤩🙌
text_content:
40 some odd years ago I was living in DC with a friend who would pick these guys up off the parkway (so they wouldn't get killed) and brought them home to our top floor brownstone. We went to the Library of Congress to find out how to care for them. Cat food with bones and eyes (yuck) worked well. They ran free and I just put papers where they liked to potty.
When I headed back to Cali, we released them in a marshy area. They couldn't run away from each other fast enough. I was dissapointed they didn't want to remain buddies.

I won't expect it to eat anything on the first day due to most likely it being stressed from being transport but I will give it a meal variety anyway.

video_title: The Most Common Mistakes Box Turtle Keepers Make & How To Avoid Them!
video_description: We all know that there is a special place in my heart for Box Turtles. I've had my turtle Franklin for 25 years and I think they make ...
text_content: I would add info on the water requirement.    I put my 7 box turtles in my bathtub  everyday or two  during those months when they were not hibernating.  Five or six inches of water.  They loved it.  Sometimes they submerged themselves.  They never got dehydrated.  I fed them a variety of food after their bath.  I had a worm farm in my refridge that I bought from WalMart.  They sucked the worms up like spaghetti and sometimes fought over the same worm.  Okay, I'm fanatical. I gave them the freedom to go anywhere in my house.  I found them in my shoes in the closet, or shelved sideways among the books in my bookshelf.  I kept them outdoors during the summer and finally released them into a remote sanctuary with a creek.  It broke my heart but I had become disabled and had no choice.  It was worse than having a child leave me with an empty nest.

video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content:
I have a really sad box turtle story from when I was a kid, as a cautionary tale. I was pretty good as far as taking care of him, I was a reptile nerd so I did research in these things called “books” that in hind sight were probably from the 70’s lol. He had a decently balanced diet of veggies, bugs and worms, and vitamin supplements. He was good natured, never bit, and often crawled toward me and other people, so he couldn’t have been that miserable lol. I had him in an aquarium with bark bedding like everyone else did back then. He had the proper lights and I was aware they liked water so I let him play in the sink almost every day. Not great husbandry, but in the late 80’s most of the info you got was from the pet store where you got them, let alone as a 10 year old kid. Anyway, my turtle loved bananas. We went on vacation, he sat down on a piece of banana we left for him- and it dried under him, and he got stuck to it on the bottom of the aquarium. He couldn’t get to his water dish and he died. An awful death to be sure. 

Use these excellent resources to do a better job than we knew to back in the day, they are excellent pets. I personally think they’re fine for beginners, mainly because their temp needs are minimal, and their food is relatively cheap and easily attainable. Gone are the days of throwing a goldfish in a bowl and having them live for 6 months, and our pets are much better off for it.

video_title: My Box Turtle Care Guide! How I Take Care Of My Oldest Pet (Learn From My Mistakes)
video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I have a Desert Box Turtle named Diego. She showed up to my back porch full grown with dog chew holes on her shell about 25 years ago and we've been best friends ever since. She's my childhood pet and I still have her living in my room for winter with all her lights in a 75 Gallon Tank. I move her tank to an outside enclosed porch around April because she hates refrigerated air and bring her back inside the first week of October. I never let her play outside alone unless I'm with her because I don't want to lose her. She's about the size of a McDonalds Cheeseburger. I have to admit that when I first got her I had no idea what I was doing, or if she had a family that she never returned to and I feel bad about that but now I'm commited to her because we moved over a decade ago and she came with. I have no idea how she got into the yard because the yard was completely fenced off and there were no holes under the fence. She just appeared at the back door. Now that she's getting older she's very active and happy and healthy but I can't get her to eat anything other than bananas (with bird vitamins 2x a week) and hamburger meat. She loves those but will absolutely not accept any other food. I feed her every day even if she doesn't eat but it's offered. I take her for a yearly check-up every beginning of October at the veterinarian and the doctor says she looks healthy. We're not sure how old she is but she was full grown when she joined my family. I'm Autistic and don't have any children or a wife of my own and I wonder if my childhood beloved pet is going to outlive me. What's she going to do if I die before her? I feel that releasing her into the wild (especially after decades in captivity and in a totally different location) is unthinkable after being in captivity for so long and having her needs taken care of but what's she going to do if I die? I wonder if there's any rescues in New Mexico. Hopefully I don't die anytime soon but she just very well may outlive me. That's a problem.

text_content: I recently purchased a baby Eastern Box Turtle and 2 baby Eastern Painted Turtles. The painteds are thriving in a 40 gallon tank right now and spend their days basking, swimming, and foraging on the bottom. One of them likes to hide in the live plants, but the other is much more confident and stays out in the open. The boxie on the other hand is in a terrestrial tank with a Zoomed waterfall, hydroball aquifer, eco-earth/cypress mulch substrate, and reptisun 80w. The boxie ate a few red wrigglers the first few days, but now doesn't eat at all. He ignores them, ignores small spotted roaches, meal worms, pellets, fresh greens and strawberries. He just won't eat. I put him in a plastic tote (no water though) with a red wriggler, spotted roach and meal worm and he just went to a corner. I've dumped about a dozen worms into his tank where they've just went into the soil, along with about 8-10 roaches, and a few meal worms, and none are to be seen now and must've all just burrowed into the substrate. Today, for the first time in 10 days the boxie found the waterfall on his own and stood under the water. So, would it be a good idea to simplify things and put him in a small tub like shown in the video for awhile until he seems to be eating regularly? The one mistake I see I was making after watching your video was not supplying enough water (there was a clay dish filled with water I sat him in a few times and the small water fall stream coming down a bark face and falling onto a gravel bed) and thinking maybe he needed higher temps to stimulate his metabolism. I'm going to raise the height of the reptisun to lower the temp a little (82° on the soil and 92° on the rock were the temps). Also, according to the video, I could also just place him in the 8" deep 40 gallon tank with the similarly sized painteds? He would be just fine swimming and walking on the bottom and using the stairs and floating basking site to get out?

video_title: World’s Most Personable Turtle! #shorts
video_description: Watch more Otis here: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLodsosc0i5TLSjx3PjX5avevTDv-lq2A5 #shorts Subscribe to our channel: ...
text_content:
I had Stubby, an eastern box turtle for 15 years. I learned a lot about turtles from her. She stayed in my room at night & in the morning I carried her out to the pen in the backyard for sunlight & to splash in the pond. One morning, halfway to the back door the phone rang. I sat her down to answer it. (Phones used to be connected to the wall.) After the call, I started doing something else. Ten minutes later I remembered Stubby. Went back to the hallway. No Stubby. She'd walk from the bath to my room, so I figured she went back there. Looked in the closet, under the bed, under the dresser. No Stubby. Checked the adjacent bedroom. No Stubby. The bathroom. Nope. Went thru the dining room. The living room. Nothing. Glanced toward the kitchen & saw a reflection in the leaf of the kitchen table. No, I thought to myself. That's not possible. There, sitting at the back door was Stubby, waiting to go out. That evening, when I brought her back inside, I sat her on the floor by the door & said what I always said when I brought her inside. Time to go to bed, Stubby. And I followed her as she walked thru the kitchen, the dining room down the hall & into my room! All those times I carried her out & in she watched where I went & learned where to go.

In the winter time, she sat by the floor heater to get warm then amble off to her spot under the bed. She loved tuna on whole wheat. She particularly enjoyed the crust. Ate it like a worm. Had ice cream, yogurt, whatever I ate. She loved strawberries, watermelon & yellow apples. Preferred earthworms to meal worms.

When I took her camping, she'd find a cubbyhole in the back of my truck & at night when I rolled out the sleeping bag she'd come in a nestle next to me. It was like sleeping with a cold rock until she warmed up. I was concerned she might suffocate, but next morning she moved out to her niche again. If I put her on a dirt trail, she'd follow my feet. Every few minutes she'd stop & periscope her surroundings. When she did more stopping than walking, I'd carry her back to the truck.

If I sat on the floor, she'd come over & gently pluck at my shorts or shoelaces wanting attention. She loved having her head stroked. 

I miss Stubby. She, too, was one in a million. She passed away in her sleep one wiinter. That was over 30 years ago. 

I've had a lot of turtles in my life & all have been special in their own way. Daisy, a western box turtle, liked to ride on my foot; Touche & Tammy, a pair of eastern box, always laid a clutch in the rose garden & every spring there were five or six hatchlings. Tulip, a western box started the parade of turtles as my first. Rescued Moon, the largest & roundest western box, from being stuck in a redwood fence.  She mated with Pansy & I have photos of her digging a nest & laying a dozen round eggs under the light of the moon & a flashlight. But Stubby showed me the intelligence, love, patience & determination of turtles. I remain hopeful that we will all meet again in that eternal campground & our adventures will continue.

Otis is like a meteor. By happenstance he came into your life. Whoever had him before didn't appreciate him. He knows he is safe with you & decided to show you his true personality! Shine on, Otis. You are indeed special. Moreover, Otis knows he is special, as turtles are very wise.

text_content: Great video on explaining your feeding set-up.  I'm in Texas and have multiple Ornate's and agree 100% that they should be kept outside.  Hibernation and all.  I was interested in your enclosure walls - they are not electrified and seem to have to "deterrent" for predators.  Where I am, my enclosure is under constant watch by skunks and armadillo's.  They don't want to eat the adults, but dig up my nests.  For the longest time I thought it was a racoon, but after installing night vision video camera's, low and behold, it was skunks and even armadillo's.  I could not figure out how they were getting in and after moving the camera's around and painstakingly watching them (8 hours of nightime video watching is boring....), I finally figured it out and stopped it - but the damage was done.  I used to keep the babies in the main enclosure but exactly like you said, they were getting picked off by hawks.  Again, the vidoe camera revealed all.  So, back my question - no deterants?  Also, do you now attract vermin like rats etc bu leaving ANY food out at night?  I used to and after watching rats and opposums eat the food, I stopped doing that.

text_content: I have a horsefield tortoise (or Russian tortoise for you guys in the states) who is just like Otis! If I take him out of his enclosure he will just follow me everywhere and lifts himself up and stretches his neck out as much as he can towards me, and interestingly he NEVER bites me (only my shoes) he's just interested in what I'm doing, but he will constantly try to nip at my girlfriend, so he clearly has preferences. The funny thing is that when I feed him I have to leave the room, even if he can't see me he will not eat while I'm there, he'll just scratch relentlessly against the wall of his enclosure until I pay him attention, so I can only assume he knows my scent, because I've tried just sitting quietly in the room and he still goes nuts as if he knows I'm there. I wish I fully understood why he's so obsessed with me and keen to interact, but I'm not complaining. I personally believe that it's because when we got him he was quite sick - he barely ate or moved and certainly had metabolic bone disease, his shell was pliable and he was super skinny, and I wasn't working at the time due to lockdown so I put all of my time into nursing him back to health. I hand fed him, bathed him in water treated with calcium and vitamins every day, and even gently held up his shell to help him learn how to walk properly until he was strong enough to lift it on his own, so I'm convinced that he somewhat understands that I'm the reason that he's alive and that's why he is the way he is with me.

text_content:
Years ago, I had a weird situation. I told Person not to put the tortoise in the pond.  It is a land animal, not a turtle.  He couldn’t understand that. Things he does like are a certain brand of Pleco Wafers for sucker fish, sometimes he eats full sized frozen shrimp. he'll eat Mole-Crickets when we catch them and toss em in the tank.  His absolute favorite was pond snails one of the guys brought in, he ate 45 of them within 2 hours.  Snail-mageddon!  I've bought many different "turtle foods" from amazon but most of them he wont eat.

text_content: I love this video and I have one of these guys! It is an adult female in a 40 gallon breeder. I will tell you one thing though with your setup and you may have made one or not but put a screen lid on the tank. I tell you this because when they do bask your dock looks pretty close to the top of the tank. They can escape their tank. My female has gotten out twice. My dock was sitting lower in the tank than yours is and she still got out. It baffles me to this day how she did it. She got out one time and I did not find her for a week and I looked everywhere for her. They are great climbers and in the wild you can see them climbing tree limbs that stick up out of the water and sitting on them to bask. These limbs can have them sitting almost two feet above the water line and when spooked they drop right into the water. It is crazy but they do it. Other than that you have an awesome setup and are taking amazing care of your six stinkpots. 🤙🏼🐢🙂

text_content: Hey, firstly thank you for making your channel, you have really helped me and my common musk turtle. Anywho the main reason I am commenting is because I ended up adopting a turtle someone left after they moved out. This turtle was in shallow water when we received her and the first day when I replaced the tank I made the water deep not realising that the turtle never developed the muscles to swim and so looked like she was drowning. I removed enough water to keep it deep but high enough if she wanted to get air from standing on her tippy toes she could. So as she got more confident we started to make it deeper and now the tank is two thirds. When we are able to afford it we do upgrade each item to give her a better quality of life. She had a deform at the back of her shell that has grown out and she has grown since we got her. Hopefully this experience will be helpful to others as well.

text_content: Shell care and exercice! I make it a habit to let my turtle out at least 3-4 times a week to make sure he is comfortable walking on land getting good exercice. This is important for him because his tank is basically filled up all the way and his basking area doesn’t allow for too much walking space. I also rub some shell care on him before I let him go, to dry up while he plays. I let him run about outside for about an hour or more, keeping an eye out for birds, lizards, etc. I have a 6 month old YBS and live in Florida, and he’s thriving :)

text_content:
This specific video came up when i  searched for turtle care. My boyfriend and his family are very ... very ... particular about his turtle. Hes had it since he was a small child and his parents wanted it more than he did, but they also ... dont have much in there. 

Hes been in only water for mostly his entire life. No basking, no decor in the tank, nothing more to eat than the dry food. I bought him some gourmet food like two years ago along with a large rock decor that lets him at least dry out. Its been super hard to try and add things to the tank. Everytime i buy something to add i feel like ive insulted his parents. They try and convince me he needs nothing more than he has and hes been fine for years, and also even tried to get me to return the items i bought but i threw out the reciept so they couldnt. 

 At least he has a massive tank, and a decent filtration system. But im the only one who throws lettuce in for a treat and i feel like everything i try, makes them look at me a certain way. Though, maybe i should be harsher with them about it, i know if this relationship continues to moving in together ill get to take care of the turtle and i look forward to the day in all honesty. Im doing more research right now to see what else i can add to the tank to help the guy, and ill just keep it up. Its hard to convince someone theyve been mistreating a reptile when they dont believe they have been at all since "he seems fine." 

This turned into a rant, but i did want to let you know most of all that for all the videos of yours ive never seen, this will be the first, but not the last. Thank you. You affect the lives of many a turtle in a tank, and i want you to know you will be affecting this one.

text_content: Thank you. This is great information! We have a 10 year old captive bred Stinkpot that we adore. His shell has retained shed flakes that have built up over the past year. We had his water too warm ~79*F and since he never used the basking area we only had a UVA/UVB bulb over the water. He started coming out onto his ramp more and more and we think this was to get out of the water that was too warm. We have the water at 76*-77*F now and have a basking bulb and UVA/UVB light. We were also told in a musk turtle group that all water conditioners are toxic to turtles and we will need drain out the water in his 55 gallon setup (we have it filled about1/3 full) and to use RO or filtered water with no conditioner. We used a new extra soft toothbrush to gently work the retained flakes loose and did a 50% water change with a faucet attachment on the sink for filtered water but it took 4 hours for the water in the tank to come up to temperature because the sink water has to be cold through the filter. What are your thoughts on the safety vs risks of water conditioners such as the Tetrafauna Aquasafe turtle water conditioner or others like it? Thank you

video_description: It's time to compare these two legendary Sternotherus musk turtles; the stripeneck musk (Sternotherus Peltifer) and loggerhead ...
text_content:
My family has had a loggerheaded musk turtle since I was a young kid - back then youtube didn't exist and it was insanely hard to find any information on the turtle. It's actually super-fun to see videos of the turtle and kind of heartwarming to see other turtles of the same type acting like she did when she was young.
I'm quite surprised that you can handle them without getting the ol' bitey-attempts from the turtles, though. The first few years of her life, ours tended to threateningly open her mouth whenever she was held (by the way, she was always placed on a palm, not just held aloft...), and sometimes even try hail-mary attempts to bite where-ever she thought a finger or hand might be. 

Fun fact, we got our turtle when I was 10, and I'm now now 38 years old - which means our silly old turtle-lady is far older then they're supposed to get, at an impressive 28 years old.

video_title: MY $10,000 TURTLE TANK 🐢
text_content:
This tank is massive and absolutely beautiful! Having tank that is less than 10gal/shell length inch is a very common mistake and your turtles are lucky to have tons of room to swim and tons of privacy areas to explore. 

Some suggestions I can provide as a turtle owner (can also find this on r/turtle)
1. Eventually each turtle needs their own tank. As babies, it is ok for them to be in the same tank, but they will get aggressive before fully reaching maturity. Pay attention to neck biting as a clear sign that it is time to separate them.
2. Again with the territorial behavior, it would be better to give each turtle its own basking area and lamp. Even as babies, one of them will start bullying others for the prime basking area.
3. The lamp you had was a single dome so I’m not sure if your bulb is just a heat bulb or a heat/uvb bulb. But turtles also need uvb and people have found that combination bulbs are not reliable at providing uvb. So it would be better to have a dual bulb lamp and have a dedicated uvb bulb to prevent arthritis in your turtles.
4. I saw some suggestions for a pump, but I did see some bubbling in one of your frames so it looks like you have one. Just make sure the pump is 5-10x the volume of your tank. This is another common mistake. So if your tank is 200 gal, you want a pump that can at least pump 1000gal/h. You might have to have multiple pumps in order fulfill that flowrate. 

Hope this helps! It looks like you spare no expense for your pets so I know you will be great turtle dad!

text_content:
To anyone who has or is thinking of getting turtles (including Tony)!! You're gonna need a non-moving land space about 25% of the tank area (like a platform they can swim under with ramp) with a uv bulb (replaced every year) and heat lamp above it, and a feed bowl for foods (including veggies) dusted with calcium and vitamins, otherwise they can get some really bad deformities.

A good rule i like to follow is even if you think you know everything you need to about your animals, do lots of googling and forum-searching before getting, and every couple months do another google to see if there's anything you could do to make their life any better. That helps avoid any mistakes, and leads to peace of mind and happy animals😊


E.g. tank size requirements for 3 adult _species_ turtles, _species_ turtle diet, treats, how often to feed, adult size, etc. Whatever pops in your mind, or scroll random forums if you can't think of anything

video_title: MY $10,000 TURTLE TANK 🐢
text_content: Hey man, turtle guy here. Nice tank man really great size, most people unfortunately keep them in tanks way too small for them. But a few pointers. Its actually not healthy for them to eat too many goldfish or minnows due to an enzyme they carry. Guppies and swordtails are a healthier livefood. Next careful with the gravel cause if it can fit there mouth you risk them choking or swallowing it so I suggest play sand, which you can get a 10-20 pound bag from home depot for like $5 you just have to clean it well, or large river stones. Lastly turtles can get very territorial so if you are planning on getting more turtles they're probably gonna need their own tank rather than living all together in one. Favorite species are Red Eared sliders and Diamondback Terapins tho. Great tank and start again tho. I know you're gonna spoil them and I can't wait to see updates.

text_content:
This was an amazing introduction to the different types of tortoises and turtles out there, but also because you talk about responsibility in the first few minutes! I own a Russian (Morty the Tortie) and a Red-footed (Yurtle the Turtle) and respect them both so much as amazing reptiles! Russians don't need just a clear enclosure to not understand boundaries. Our Morty is in a dark enclosure and when he wants to get out for a walk, will repeatedly bang, scratch, and try to climb out. One thing I think you could have hit on with the Russian is that they need lots of exercise and/or a nice big enclosure, because in the wild they impressively walk 4 MILES A DAY (pretty impressive when you take their size into account!) Their also speed demons! My guy can run off in a flash, so you have to be careful to not lose sight of them. 

A big pro is their amazing personalities! Morty the Tortie reminds me of a grumpy old man who is actually lovable under their hard shell! He's very social and so smart! They're amazingly great pets, but just like you, I wish pet stores would not sell wild caught and teach buyers that they need a big enclosurer of about 6 square feet. 

Thanks so much for teaching responsibility in reptile ownership, something rare for channels because they are also breeding them. Also, I LOVE that you spoke about rescuing sulcatas. Amazing video! I subscribed instantly!

video_title: Transforming my Tiny Turtle's Tank
video_description: Me when the turtle is turtling Music: Peanut Butter Sandwich - Tiki Tiki Urban Conspiracy - Jules Gaia Freudian Slip - Bladverk ...
text_content: Ok, out of kindness here is some turtle care needs and warnings for keeping turtles, I looked though the comments and noticed some people had outdated or incorrect turtle care recommendations and stuff. I just need people to understand this before getting turtles because I had read every comment and I do not want anyone getting turtles and unknowingly putting them at risk. And the one thing that bothered me the most in the comments is “you should get more turtles” or “Get him a friend “ like idk if you guys know but NO! 👉TURTLES👏 ARE 👏NOT 👏SOCIAL👏 ANIMALS 👏AND 👏THEY 👏WILL 👏HAVE 👏A 👏HIGH 👏POSSIBILITY 👏FOR👏 HARMING👏 IF 👏NOT 👏KILLING 👏EACH 👏OTHER! 👏IF 👏YOU 👏WANT 👏MORE👏 THEN 👏ONE 👏TURLE 👏IN👏THE👏SAME👏TANK👏THEN 👏YOU 👏WOULD 👏NEED 👏A 👏LARGER 👏TANK! 👏AND 👏EVEN 👏THEN 👏THERE 👏WOULD👏 ALWAYS 👏HAVE 👏A 👏RISK 👏OF 👏THEM👏BEING 👏HURT 👏OR 👏DYING! 👈 I’M NOT SAYING TO BE RUDE, I AM SAYING THAT TO PREVENT ANY IMPULSE BUYING THAT WOULD LEAD TO A HURT OR DEAD TURTLE AND PREVENTING A NEW OWNER FROM LEARNING THE HARD WAY THAT CAN BE HEARTBREAKING! 💔 So if you get a turtle the general rule for aquatic turtles is 10 gallons of water per inch of shell length, meaning a 4-inch turtle needs a minimum 40-gallon tank, and adults often require 75-120+ gallons; PUR TURTLE, DOUBLE THAT AMOUNT FOR TWO THOUGH I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND HOMEING THEM TOGETHER! this provides enough swimming space and accounts for their growth, but remember it's a minimum, and larger is always better. Always have a basking area that a turtle can get completely dry on and with that they need heat and UVB, preferably not the conjoined double lamp heat and UVB because the UVB light  in those kits aren’t the best, instead a tube UVB light that’s a 5T light is more recommend and make sure both the heat and UVB light hit the same basking area, also a water filter is also important and because turtles are messy, I say for example if you have one baby turtle in a 20 gallon long tank get a filter that is at least met for a 40-55 gallons (that what works for me at least with the tiny baby turtle) and as your turtle grows they will need to be upgraded so if you wanna save on money look into buying second hand tanks or using storage tubs or stock tanks, you don’t even have to fill the tank up all the way if it’s still a baby, you can start with a 75 gallon tank for example and keep it shallow and just raise the water level as it grows (keep in mind the water should be deep enough for it to swim but shallow enough to easily reach the surface to breathe, typically 1.5 to 2 times its shell length, or about 4-8 inches, gradually increasing as it grows stronger, ensuring it can always stand on its hind legs and touch the bottom or easily get to the surface.) and as for plants it depends on the turtle, I have some turtles that would ignore the plants (like my Mississippi map turtle) who would eat the romaine lettuce and sliced carrots but won’t eat the guppy grass in his tank or the hornwort. Or my baby red belly cooter who would eat every plant I give her besides the hornwort. Turns out turtles in the wild don’t care for hornwort do to the texture of the plant even if it’s safe to eat. None of my turtle eat or even like hornwort so you can try hornwort if you like just keep in mind it’s a floating plant,doesn’t need substrate. Also when it comes to substrate there is many options to choose from, you can do sand, tile, no substrate, large river rocks (make sure they are too big for the turtle to eat) but I would stay away from aquarium gravel because it can cause  impaction if eaten and there is many bad stores of turtles dying from aquarium gravel. As for decoration make sure that it’s something that would NOT crush the turtles, something that wouldn’t risk the turtle getting stuck, something that does not have blade like edges,and something that is aquarium safe that the turtle won’t eat, sometimes people use plastic plants but for me personally I wouldn’t trust it because I heard some turtles nip at it thinking it’s a real plant at first and we wouldn’t want our beloved turtles to eat plastic. Another thing is food, do NOT feed turtles  goldfish, rosy red minnows(any any minnows),and other cyprinids (like common minnows, dace) away from turtles because they contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys Vitamin B1, causing neurological damage, paralysis, and death in turtles, and also avoid aggressive fish like Cichlids that can harm the turtle's eyes. Slow, colorful, or long-finned fish like Bettas, Koi, or large Plecos are also bad choices as they are easy prey or attract unwanted attention, while fast, small livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies, and mosquito fish) are better options for enrichment or occasional treats, but always monitor for safety. Also turtles need a water heater, even more so in their baby and juvenile year! As for main food pellets there is many options just try and keep it diverse and try to avoid as much of the low quality ones if possible, for me personally I use Ultra Fresh Floating Turtle food, zoo med turtle food, and mazuri turtle food. Also almost forgot but make sure you had a cuddle bone for calcium,turtles need it for their shell health. ANYWAY Please make sure to do your research before getting a turtle let alone any pet, turtles are a big commitment and can live for many years! And if one day you end up not being able to care for the turtle do NOT release it into the wild, turtles brought up in captivity can not survive and if they do they can become invasive in some areas, best to responsibility find the turtle a new home if one day you decide you don’t want it or can’t care for it anymore!

text_content: I got my Red Eared Slider Turbo when she was approx 1-2 years old, from a cousin who didn't want her anymore. That's her, my icon picture. She had to have an x-ray. I had no idea what I was in for, but I wouldn't trade her for the world. She's been with me now for 17 years, most of my life. She lives in a 110-gallon Tuff-Tub, for which I have custom-built a landing platform and heat and light rig for her. Full-spectrum + UVB + ceramic heat emitter + a canister filter rated for a 500-gallon outdoor pond. Here's what they don't tell you: when you have a turtle this big they don't make habitat supplies for you. You have to get very comfortable thinking outside the box, breaking out the power tools, and making late-night trips to your local hardware store sometimes. But I love my baby! She's smart, stubborn, the vet always complements her on her beautiful carapace, and she's afraid of precisely nothing. She's the perfect fit for me as a companion animal.

text_content:
I had a red-eared slider for about 15 years. My parents bought him (and about 3 others who didn't make it past the first month) for us at a farmer's market. I knew nothing about taking care of a turtle and did not at the time think to look for resources to better take care of him.

Over the years I got a bit better with him, but when it came time to move 600 miles, I realized that I wasn't giving him the home that he needed, and donated him to a local reptile care facility. With any luck he's living a much better life now.

The point of me saying all that is, I wish that I could have seen a video like this before getting my turtle. I would have known either to not get him at all or that I was in for a lot of work, which I ended up finding out the hard way.

Now that I'm working towards my first Ball Python, I am so, so grateful for channels like Clint's that help us learn about what it takes to take care of these wonderful animals. I hope that this video helps others learn much more easily than I did that red-eared sliders, which deserve to have great lives, are not the easiest pets to give a great life.

text_content: There’s a lot of misinformation here. Especially in the photos and videos shown. There’s a couple turtles with severe metabolic bone disease (shells curling up). Pictures of turtles being offered food out of water (they have to eat in water or they will choke). There is a photo of a turtle with a severely overgrown beak. Many of the photos/videos you show are not even red eared sliders. You responded to someone’s comment about a serious size difference in their 2 red eared sliders. It’s not a good idea to house more than 1 together because they will compete for resources and one could be winning (the dominant one). That’s not everything wrong with the video. Please don’t put out animal care videos if you don’t do the proper research.

video_title: Two headed turtle Update‼️
text_content:
Good evening.  I am so  glad your patient(s), survived the ordeal of the past few days/week.  I’ve got some gen. tips.  First, I am a retired animal keeper of a rescue zoo in PA.  Over my tenure of 30 + yrs., I’ve served as head keeper of all the depts., (domestics + exotics), mainly w/  birds, mammals and reptiles.  Anytime you put a turtle, baby or adult, in a container, please know that even a weak critter will try to escape, it gets them into real trouble as you’ve now experienced.  Set your container up to replicate nature, they will feel more that it’s their home.  We set our baby turtle tanks up w/ a  wet part and a dry part, doing that replicates nature.  Add some rocks they can scratch on, a few sticks and greens for them to eat, like duck weed, etc.  if using a plastic tank Start by scuffing
 the inner bottom of your smooth, of plastic, container, it provides traction for their nails to catch on to right themselves.  

It would be better to not house  turtles  on a plain, smooth tank bottom, they need enrichment in their habitat, like they’d have in nature, they live near and in fresh water ponds, they haul out during the day to sun themselves.  Gather together several pounds to of local dirt & clay, then use the clay  for a bottom layer, across the entire container.  Mix the dirt & clay together, to make a higher level and an even higher mound for 3;4 of the container.  It’s been our experience the 2 head turtles always want to do different things, even fight the other head, constantly flipping itself over, as you’ve seen.  Enriching the enclosure will help them from boredom and searching for a real pond.  To keep babies safe we use a piece of wood, a 1” x 6” x width of enclosure, of untreated wood, we use the board to divide the water side from the dry side, when they are unattended by humans, never leave them unattended on the water side.   We soak daily 10;15 min, several times a day.  After our babies are done soaking, swimming, etc., we put in the dividing board, to prevents accidents in case one flips another over or themselves over in the water,.  baby Turtles can drown, even in 1/4” water.   Also, turtles need plenty of Sun or, reptile light source, be careful though, if your glass tank is in front of a window, the Sun can overheat them fast, use a shade cloth if tank is in direct Sun or in front of a window, especially in Summer with Southern exposure.  

Soak them instead, but never leave them alone in any water.  At their stage offering water via dish is tricky for a  turtle with 2 heads, if you add a small water dish bury it in the dirt mound so it is is flush with the dirt level.  We use a teeny syringe, to offer water that way we can monitor each heads water intake.  Also a wet rag in a corner of tank is helpful, they can mouth it to moisture, while you are away.  Your turtle can also get water from foods you feed like crushed fish, plants, greens, damp turtle pellets, etc.  it’s a good idea to weigh your turtle weekly.  Good luck to you on this journey, the 2 headed turtle can be difficult to manage.   If you need help, there’s many online success stories, don’t hesitate to reach out to the folks keeping duel headed turtles alive and thriving.

for a bottom layer across the entire container mix the dirt clay together to make a higher level and an even higher mound for 3 4 of the container it s been our experience the 2 head turtles always want to do different things even fight the other head constantly flipping itself over as you ve seen enriching the enclosure will help them from boredom and searching for a real pond to keep babies safe we use a piece of wood a 1 x 6 x width of enclosure of untreated wood we use the board to divide the water side from the dry side when they are unattended by humans never leave them unattended on the water side we soak daily 10 15 min several times a day after our babies are done soaking swimming etc we put in the dividing board to prevents accidents in case one flips another over or themselves over in the water baby turtles can drown even in 1 4 water also turtles need plenty of sun or reptile light source be careful though if your glass tank is in front of a window the sun can overheat them fast use a shade cloth if tank is in direct sun or in front of a window especially in summer with southern exposure soak them instead but never leave them alone in any water at their stage offering water via dish is tricky for a turtle with 2 heads if you add a small water dish bury it in the dirt mound so it is is flush with the dirt level we use a teeny syringe to offer water that way we can monitor each heads water intake also a wet rag in a corner of tank is helpful they can mouth it to moisture while you are away your turtle can also get water from foods you feed like crushed fish plants greens damp turtle pellets etc it s a good idea to weigh your turtle weekly good luck to you on this journey the 2 headed turtle can be difficult to manage if you need help there s many online success stories don t hesitate to reach out to the folks keeping duel headed turtles alive and thriving

I have had my tortoise for 29 years now - He is 44 years old😊 At first there wasn’t internet and in the books it was not always correct information.

Could you tell me about your substrate, because I’ve tried a lot of things and there comes bugs and different kind of animals in it even if I clean of it of pee and poo. What do you do, to keep these away? And underneath it what have you put there?

About bath, I only do it once in a while, if I can see he can’t push his poo out, or if he has a lot of pee on him. As you said it’s not normal for them in the wild. I put him in the bath room sink he loves water smoothly running over him, he loves that and then afterwards little water he can sit in. They must only be in there max 15-20 min. because otherwise they can get worm I have read. I only tooth brush his behind quickly, and if you notice your tortoise doesn’t seem to like the tooth brush, and mine doesn’t either, so I only do it shortly on his behind to remove poo etc. on his behind. 

About food it has been hard for me to get him to eat other things, because at first I didn’t know about all of these things and he got a lot he shouldn’t have. Earlier on I went about two weeks, (they can live without food about three weeks), where he didn’t eat the other things, and he “won” but now I put it on top on his food and hope he gets more used to it, but he is sneaky and try his best to avoid it, if it’s possible😊

video_title: RUSSIAN TORTOISES ARE THE BEST BEGINNER PET REPTILE!
video_description: Guys! I did it! I found the best beginner reptile! Let me explain my reasoning as to why a Russian Tortoise is the best for beginners ...
text_content:
Wholeheartedly agree on your point about having to be passionate about keeping any reptile. A dog is easy, you just have to provide them with one of thousands of good food products that are readily available in any supermarket, a place to sleep, a daily walk and to train them in basic things like not going to the toilet indoors - you have to be particularly useless to be unable to meet their basic needs. Horsefield/Russian Tortoises don't require the same amount of attention as a dog and are far cheaper to care for, but they're still far more complex in terms of their basic needs and a lot of people don't do their research before buying them.

The unfortunate thing with their popularity as a "beginner reptile" and how easily available they are in comparison with other reptiles is that a lot of the time parents get them for their kids as a first pet thinking they're on a similar level to a hamster or guinea pig in terms of care, they don't realise how much work actually goes into the husbandry and often the kids get bored of them within a year and want a dog so you end up finding hundreds of poorly looked after tortoises being sold online that have health issues like MBD from lack of UVB, poor diet and being kept on improper substrate and they commonly perish at a far too young age from more serious problems like respiratory issues or even just stress from not having their basic needs met. The shop were I got my first one from didn't even give any information on how to care for them or ask if we had the right set up, I just walked in, pointed at the one I wanted, paid and walked out of the shop with it - luckily I'd already done my research, but I do worry about how many people do just walk in there and buy one on a whim without knowing a single thing about them. Whenever I've spoken to someone who's thinking about getting a tortoise I always recommend that they get one from a dedicated reptile shop or a trusted breeder who won't sell one to you unless you can prove you have the right set up and will provide you with a care sheet.

text_content: Clint, I appreciate this,  I have read and watched anything I can on these as it's my favorite  turtle! I legitimately  was just saying there isn't a lot of information on the mata mata, and I wake up to this. I  rescued a baby mata mata from someone local here who kept him in a plastic container with no filter barely fed. I have had him two years now in a pond setup, I do use a sand, media sump and uv filter.  The room stays very humid as the frogs and geckos I keep require it and I keep it temp regulated, my ph stays around 6.0 to 6.5 he feeds on different live feeders, I do use a supplement with as well. Most <1m pythons are great for beginners because they don’t see you as food. Pythons usually use a wrap and strangle method to kill prey, and that means if they don’t see you as viable food, they won’t try to hurt you for the most part. 

Just remember, snakes have their own temperament, just like any other animal. If your snake prefers not to be handled very much or something like that, don’t torture it. I know snake owners who don’t ever interact with their snake other than to feed and change water because the snake gets stressed out. Pythons are known as big one brain cell sweeties, but not all of them are comfortable with always being around you or out of the enclosure much.

video_title: DO NOT Get A Pet Ball Python Until You Watch This!
video_description: Ball Pythons are the best pet reptiles right? Well, just because Royal Pythons are the most popular pet snake doesn't mean they ...
text_content:
I've had my ball python for almost a year now and there's some stuff I wish I'd have known before hand! I did a tonne of research but ultimately if you decide you're equipped to keep one well then you just have to go for it and learn through physical experience. 

The main thing I wish I'd known is how quickly they grow. I got my guy when he was 6 months old, and in less than a year he's pretty much outgrown his tank and now I'm in the market for a new one. I shouldn't have wasted my money on a smaller "grow out" viv! Big vivs are expensive and take up a lot of space, and 100% necessary. 

They are sensitive, if husbandry is off or if they are sick they won't eat. A lot of people put missed meals down to "typical" BP behavior rather than checking their snake's home and parameters, or getting them checked by a reptile vet. Yes some do go off food, but its much safer to not assume and investigate everything before assuming its normal! 

They are expensive because of their size - the current cost of living crisis here is causing a lot of people to abandon their pets, snakes included. Make sure you have the resources to cover every eventuality before you take one in! (that goes for every pet tbh)

I love my pal, and don't regret him at all. I'm looking forward to a hopefully long and healthy life with him! But I feel sometimes reptiles fall victim to the same treatment as fish, people think you can buy a tank and just throw them in and not bother with them and provide 0 effort. I'm glad these kind of videos exist and that we're finally changing attitudes towards their welfare!

video_title: My Top 10 Reasons Ball Pythons Make Great Pets #ballpython #royalpython #reptiles #reptile
text_content:
Got my first 2 snakes last year for my birthday, I’ve always wanted one. My dad had bought them from a pet store and I didn’t realize until a like 3 weeks later they hate mites. My heart dropped, I didn’t know anything about mites and I looked it up and tried treating them myself and quarantined them separately for about 1 almost 2 months while using mite spray on them once a day. I stopped seeing the mites so I took them off of quarantine. They seemed perfectly fine but maybe about a week later, I found lots of mites in their tank (which I thoroughly cleaned before I let them out of quarantine) I did more researches and I found out they had a respiratory infection I’m not sure how they got it since their tank was always clean. Me and my parents search around but there was no exotic vet near us, the ones that were, were closed. I was basically balling my eyes out for days (I’m tearing up as I type this) they sadly passed away. A month later, I bought a snake from a reptile expo. I have given her new things and threw out all of the old supplies I’ve had and cleaned very well before her arrival and also brought a new tank (at the reptile expo, I was meant to buy my old snakes 2 tanks before they were gone) but now I am happy with my ball python who will be turning 1 year next month, she’s very healthy and happy and everything is going good, I’d clip a picture of her if I could.

Moral of the story,
Don’t buy reptiles from a pet store, they only want your money and probably don’t know the first thing to care for them… they didn’t even know my snakes age or gender which was the first red flags 🚩🚩

text_content:
Our Ivory girl, Luna, has been a joy to have.  We were told she was vicious and unpredictable, and that she would only eat live.  Well, she’s absolutely covered in scars from that kind of thinking.  She took a f/t rat her first week with us, and has never struck at us.  She hisses sometimes if we annoy her.  Lol

My son has autism and she has helped him,  for sure be more responsible, but also to come out of his shell and improve his speech, dexterity, and reflexes.  It was a pleasant surprise.  🥰

text_content:
Oh what timing, been for the past several days going deep into reptile research, and last night and today been talking with a friend about ball python morphs/morph combos we really want! Very likely going ball python as my "first" (in quotes because technically years ago when I was in early elementary my mom and I had snakes and frogs, but he care was... Very suboptimal) snake, because they are what I want in a snake (being a slower, thick bodied snake that's not too big and has such pretty varieties), plus the fact they are so hardy and so available! text_content:
I absolutely love berms, the ones I've known who have been handled were all sweethearts, and I've helped people get pretty decent sized ones (15ish foot was the largest berm) get used to being held. Even if they start off highly defensive (more helpful to keep in mind when a snake strikes it's a defensive act rather than aggressive one in most cases unless they honestly think your body part is food), I've found with daily work and proper care they tend to chill out relatively quickly.

The hardest one I helped a friend work with was her slightly larger reticulated python her son never touched then abandoned with her. She couldn't handle him but wanted to do her best to keep him 'cause she felt it wasn't his fault. His enclosure wasn't the best size, but once he calmed down he was usually chilling outside it.

I may love the actual giant snakes, but after a certain point size becomes the limiter, but it isn't just for size and danger level the way many think, after a certain size their feed animals get tricky. I mean- you can go to meat markets and farms to get prekilled animals telling them not to butcher the meat for you, but it gets tricky depending on where you live... even when they don't eat nearly as often as smaller snakes.

I wouldn't want to get an animal who need to eat feed animals larger than a rat, ideally frozen/thawed- and I feel bad about that 'cause I love rats as pets... unless it was fish. Far easier to go to a fish market to get whole fish of varying sizes if you're anywhere near port cities. SO easy.

So yeah- I'd sooner have an adult alligator where they can eat a wider range of meats and I wouldn't have to feed whole mammals and whole chickens consistently- though as any other reptile they need a varied diet, than a reticulated python- but to be fair, I wouldn't have alligators either.


Though I have known someone with a young alligator and "he" was awesome- so the temptation when I was told one could be gotten for me if I wanted was greater than when I was offered a breeding pair of anacondas for free.

Though won't lie- was actually tempted at the idea of them, my brain went to turning my basement apartment into a giant snake room with the sizable bedroom turned into a pool with a waterfall for them... then I thought "Nah- I really shouldn't own anything that could kill me..." and turned them down.


Edited to add- yeah- that part with you telling people what to do is needed if you aren't with people who know- when I helped with larger snakes where it goes into "I may not die but my ribs are breaking if not other bones..." mode and up I just have an open bottle of Listerine or a strong drinking alcohol around in case something happened and I get incapacitated and seriously have seconds to get it off- I think what people forget or don't think of is every time you breathe out they constrict more, so when you try to breathe in again you can't and so on until... ugh- I hate the idea of hurting a snake, but alcohol is one of the "easier" ways with a high likelyhood of success of getting a snake disengaged- that and grabbing the tail and pulling back can make continued constricting much harder for them, if not impossible- depending on the size.

Very very intentionally I've topped out my handling of the large snakes at 15 feet, I want 2 people with me at a min, esp if I know they're quirky- the alcohol of the non-rubbing kind uncapped and ready for someone to grab, and a phone close enough so 911 can be called. Only a few times I only had 1 other person, and a few times whoever was with me wasn't experienced. I have no interested with working with larger on any kind of regular basis...

What do you mean Burmese pythons don’t make good pets?
I was on board with you up until you said that.
That is absolutely false information sir.
Yes they are big snakes, yes some people shouldn’t own them if they are small. And don’t mean that because they will be eaten I mean that because it’s a heavy snake. Now, if you’re 4’8” and 75lb soakin wet ya you could be mistaken for prey long enough it could be bad.
But a full grown average sized human is perfectly capable of handling these animals.

For anyone reading this that doesn’t know a lot about Burmese, please understand that these snakes are known to be one of the most docile big snakes that there are. You can literally pet their head and it will watch you and be perfectly fine with it. They are super intelligent and you can see it.

If you like snakes, and wouldn’t mind a big snake, a Burmese python is a great choice.

Don’t let the stuff in Florida scare you. People there will take 5 others with them and jump on big snakes grabbing their head. What do expect the snakes reaction to be? Duh..
You can’t blame a snake, it’s not their fault what they do they are truly creatures of instinct.

text_content: My only concern with recommending anyone get a burmese python is the amount of space required for their enclosure. Yeah, you're right that the actual care isn't too hard, but I don't think most people understand or are able to supply the vast amount of space needed for them to live comfortably and thrive. We're talking like 4 ft by 8 ft floor space at the least for an adult. They can be such wonderful and active snakes when given the room they need, but often I see them housed in small tubs or small cages where they can really only have enough room to coil up and sit in a corner all day. I just hope you can provide the space needed and I'm hoping your new place provides that space. :)

video_description: Twitch Livestreams! http://twitch.tv/newenglandreptile http://twitch.tv/donnyrapture ⭐️10% off frozen food! RodentPro.com Code: ...
text_content: I have kept king cobras, retics, and Burmese pythons, and will have to agree on the first two's intelligence level. Kings don't go into spastic mode like many other cobras or venomous snakes do. They observe and measure you , appearing to judge your intentions based on what or how you are moving. One has to keep these animals to fully understand what I am talking about here.  Retics, on the other hand, are inquisitive; sometimes when you open up their cage and see their heads turning and tongue flicking in your direction, you'd think that a bite would be forthcoming once you pick them up, as that is what usually happens with a lot of other snakes. You don't really see that behavior in Burms unless they are anticipating food.  Also with the Burmese, you can start picking them up immediately upon opening the enclosure, but with most retics, you will have to feel them first and try to gauge their mood before reaching out.  Not saying that retics are more predisposed to aggression, it's just that they go through mood swings far more frequently than the Burmese.

text_content:
My very first snake is a lesser banana ball python. I took him from someone who could no longer keep him and it is the best impulse buy I've ever made. He's a real sweetheart! He'll always approach the glass whenever I am close to it, and he tries to climb onto my glasses or head whenever he gets the chance. He is very curious and always trying to explore his surroundings. He's a very good eater, but he's still young so that might change. I have him in a bioactive setup (Terra Firma from the BioDude) with an Arcadia deep heat projector and an UVB light. 

I'd love a burmese python, but being a single woman that probably wouldn't be the best idea. Burmballs are gorgeous but I'm not rich enough for those.

Its better to spend a little bit longer, waiting until you've researched the crap out of a more intermediate animal and waiting until you are ready for the animal you want, then settling for an animal you dont want.

text_content: Had an albino Burmese Python many years ago; she was absolutely beautiful and my God what a big baby. She absolutely loved being around people, she would slither over to the couch and come up to lay with you. I eventually married, had children, and had to move to a location which made it difficult to buy food for her; this was prior to the internet so I couldn’t just order it like today. I reluctantly gave her to a friend of mine who was a veterinarian. He raised alligators, iguanas, and tegus so she was in great hands. My wife, daughter, and I would go visit her regularly.  He kept her for many years and even bred her before she finally passed away. She was a beast, it generally took two of us to move her around for habitat renovations, etc.

text_content: I wish more people would give Carpet Pythons the time of day.  Irian Jaya Carpet pythons are basically as hardy as a corn snake and top out around 6ft. That was my wifes 1st snake 9 years ago and now she keeps retics and all sorts of stuff. Anyways, IJ carpet pythons have surprisingly low humidity requirements compared to other members of Morelia. They also do not need it particularly hot. Low to Mid 80s is plenty for the warm side. Unlike some carpet python localities they only really need to be misted if they are shedding. My male hasnt skipped a meal in 9 years. One of the easiest snakes i own. Even my ball python technically takes more work because i have to mist her and she likes to skip meals. It is probably true that it is harder to get bitten by a ball python than a carpet python, but my Carpet python hasnt ever bitten anyone either. He has a fantastic feeding response, but as long as you use your head its easy to manage. He is tap trained and all that too. I mostly keep Burms, retics, and an Afrock. So hes kind of like the training snake since he acts very similarly to the retics.

Yes, they are pretty,

video_title: Asian Water Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The Asian water monitor (Varanus salvator) is one of the biggest, most beautiful, most awesome, and common monitors that you ...
text_content:
I actually interned at a zoo for awhile that kept this species.

Only reptile I’ve met that was possibly even friendlier than a bearded dragon. Our’s LOVED people, and reminded me of a giant puppy. Would follow you around the enclosure when you cleaned it in the hope you had food. Never tried to bite or attack anyone. Would come up to the front of the enclosure and sit there for hours because it liked to watch all the guests- and most of our mammals didn’t even do that. 

Wicked smart, one of the keepers did target training with it to make moving it/vet visits easier, and to keep it’s brain occupied.

BUT due to their enormous size there’s very few people I would ever recommend this to.
It’s one of those animals that basically needs it’s own room in your house, and honestly even that likely isn’t big enough. And that’s being conservative on space, not being one of those people that recommend huge enclosures for any random animal. Certain people/organizations can properly keep them and should be allowed to do so, but I wouldn’t casually recommend them to most people.

Cost on the CBB are too expensive, ranging from $500 up to $12,000, and with minimal handling can become nervous and flighty with risk of bitting just like an import or wild caught. text_content:
I had one of these, impulse rescue buy from a store as she had infected feet that weren’t being treated. No idea how to keep them really (this was almost 50 years ago) She was a little smaller than this one but still pretty big. Temperament wise I got really lucky, though she only bonded to me and would hiss and threaten to tail whip visitors if she felt like it. Never hurt me in any way. She would lie across the back of the sofa behind me, her only sin was stealing fried eggs off my plate if I was eating 😄 I could hold her and she would go to sleep.
She once bit the vet that was treating her feet…somehow grabbed her finger at the first joint and refused to let go. She finally did, poor vet went to ER!
She was wild caught I’m sure. Passed away after only a year or two, feet never really properly healed and I think she had the fatty liver too. I wish I had her now, with knowledge we have. She was so intelligent and affectionate with me.
I had half a room for her enclosure but she had run of the house most of the time.

To note, an ackie will need a large enclosure...prepare to spend between $500-$1500 on the enclosure. The ackie itself will likely cost less than the enclosure. Luckily, they seem to breed well so there is rarely a time where you can't find one from a good breeder.

The only "knock" on ackies are they do need a fairly large enclosure for their size. Other than that, they may simply be the best pet lizard period all things considered.

video_title: Feeding a Savannah Monitor! 🦎 Why These Lizards Go Wild for Food😅
video_description: Feeding time with my Savannah monitor is always an adventure In this video, you'll see just how wild these lizards can get ...
text_content:
This is exactly why most people do not understand the correct way to keep Savannah monitors ! It’s because of Videos like this one giving the wrong information ! Just for the record…I’ve been keeping Savannahs for 5-6yrs now…& that’s 5-6yrs of doing research and taking care of them the correct way ! There is absolutely no reason why any monitors especially Savannahs that are strictly insectivores… Are being fed a grocery store diet… to include ground turkey, chicken gizzards or chicken legs which is what this poor Savannah was being fed in this video…. Which I’m sure is the main reason why it’s overweight  ! Whole pray items are the best to feed monitors & it’s actually cheaper to do so ! Not to mention, way more healthier for the animal ! 
Savannahs are strictly insectivore & in the wild they are mainly eating the large African land snails, millipedes & whatever other insects they can find. If fed the incorrect diet… like grocery store items..Savannahs & other monitors Will develop fatty liver disease pretty quick & we live a very short life 😢😣
If anyone is wanting a Savannah… please do your research first & get the correct care information. Don’t take notes from videos like this ! 
Your Savannah will thank you !

video_title: BABY Southern Alligator Lizard Care Guide | 2021
video_description: A comprehensive care guide on the Southern Alligator Lizard! Enjoy! - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - #alligatorlizard #careguide ...
text_content: Pretty good care info, but they do like humidity as they usually live near water.  Southern Alligator Lizards ( Elgaria )  are semi arboreal and like to climb. My baby is in a tall viv and is always up high basking and hunting. You did not mention UVB and they DO need it for bone health and health. They need a low UVB 5% or the new Arcadia arboreal shadedweller kit.  There are breeders and taking an adult from the wild is not such a great thing. They are aggressive and stress out and are probably carrying parasites and when stressed can make them sick. Always take a fecal sample to your reptile vet to make sure.  Babies are delicate but hardy and will take to being handled much better. Keep temps on the lower side even for the basking area. They can dehydrate easy especially babies.  I had one for 15 years and this is my second one. I also have its Mexican cousin ,  A Mexican Alligator lizard ( Abronia Graminae)  Bright green with yellow eyes and have had her for over a year.

video_title: I Recommend Pet Crocodiles FOR BEGINNERS! The Safest "Crocodilian" Pets!
video_description: Alligators and Crocodiles make TERRIBLE Pets for most reptile keepers right? These 5 Crocodilian pets make amazing pets even ...
text_content:
I have a crocodile gecko that I caught in my bedroom (they're common in my country, Portugal). They are very shy of human contact, specially if they're wild caught but they're so cute and cool looking. They're small and don't require a lot of care like other lizards, they're pretty hardy too. They just need a vertical terrarium cause they like to climb and also having caves/rocks/trunks on the floor so they can hide. Mine spends all day hidden inside a little cave and comes out at sunset/night. Sometimes she's out during the day but it's rare. They can drop their tail too and regrow it but it won't look as good as the original. From the experience I have with my crocodile gecko she won't drop her tail so easily, I've handled her while cleaning the terrarium and and when I moved her from terrarium and I could tell she was scared and stressed out cause of my presence and touch but she never dropped her tail which I think is a good sign. Even then, they'll often hide very quickly if they see people even from afar.
Ps: this is my experience with a wild caught and doesn't mean it applies to other geckos from the same species, specially if they're bred in captivity but I hope it helps. Also she was still a juvenile when caught and so far (2 years later), luckily, she's doing fine with no appearant issues.

Also a good tip for beginners.
Dont get a baby, get an subadult or adult frog.
You cant really treat parasites on frogs, so when you get a baby thats full of worms cause the breeder doesn keep an eye on it it will probably die.
If the breeder or store managed to raise it to a certain size, its a good sign that the animal is healthy and the seller knows what he/she is doing.

text_content:
For beginners, all amphibians that require either a lot of climbing space or a filtered water feature that isnt a water ball is probably not that great. Thats why a lot of toads are just great! I think most of them dont climb that much and water bowl is almost always good enough. Plus they are very easy to feed (biggest problem is overfeeding them). Add to that the price for them which is usually quite low and the price of their enclosure (which can be very very cheap for toads that require no additional heating).

text_content:
As a frog keeper, there's a problem I can identify: it seems the more engaging the frog is, the more downsides it has. For example, pixie frogs, pacman frogs, tomato frog and toads are quite easy as far as frogs go; fairly available, easy to house, inexpensive. On the other hand, these sorts of frogs are kinda boring. On the other end of the spectrum, dart frogs are incredibly wonderful to observe; absolutely beautiful, endearing and active. However, in addition to being particularly fragile, they are microphagists which is annoying. Tree frogs also have some liabilities, while also being very rewarding. I don't mind since I'm willing to put up with fruit flies and I still find joy in the more sedate frogs, but its these reasons why frogs in general aren't the best starter herps.

The solution therefor is arguably the tiger salamander. Housing them is easy, terrestrial salamander care is fairly similar to that of toads and other terrestrial frogs, they're big and colorful, and they have a very engaging personality, reputably begging for food and otherwise acting like leopard geckos. Except they aren't widely available captive bred...

text_content:
Please do not get any more frogs until you know something about them. Firstly, a ten gallon is way to small for those species. A twenty gallon is okay for them but the bigger the better. Secondly, cohabing different species in such a small tank is careless. The blue one is often hard to cohab anyways so two different tanks for them is the best. Third, before feeding you should have some calcium and vitamin powder on the flies. Fourthly, you should not touch them with your bare hands. They have very sensitive skin and although they aren’t toxic to you, you can have stuff on your hands that can be harmful to them. Lastly, they like to climb so 
have corkbark or other things for 
them too climb on. Im not trying to ruin anything for you but please get some more knowledge about dart frogs so they actually can enjoy their home. Hope you take this into consideration if you ever want to get more frogs. Bonus tip, get a hygrometer so you always know when to water them.

text_content:
Remember, you gotta feed it and look after it. And there's the risk it can attack others (huge lawsuit/prison) or attack you instead (disabled/dead).

For those reasons:
1- Flying Squirrel (pretty cute, somewhat cool, not dangerous or expensive to look after)
2- Hermit Crab (even cheaper and easier to own)
3- Ostrich (okay, you will need a farm. Kinda dangerous but manageable)
4- Tiger (no way. You will basically become a zoo keeper)
5- Gorilla (cheaper and less dangerous than a Tiger, but note in many places Gorilla's have rights similar to Humans. So their care and habitat is to a much higher standard, meaning you have to be extremely wealthy to even consider this. Much more so than a Tiger).

video_title: How Raccoons (Almost) Got Away With It
video_description: Nessie's Linktree- https://linktr.ee/Lochnessofficial TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@lochnessofficial?_r=1&_t=ZT-93hL3Ntn1mY ...
text_content:
EDITED TO ADD
I wasn’t expecting so many reactions, so could you do me a favor and read through the thread before asking questions or making comments to see if yours has already been answered? I’m repeating myself a lot. 

Exotic mammals veterinarian here. I treated pet and wild raccoons for three decades and I’m here to tell you the babies are super-cute and friendly. BUT…once they reach adulthood, most of them just want you dead. I saw more owners get bitten by raccoons than any other pet. You have to sedate them to do literally anything to them, even brush them.

They’re smart, they’re cute, they’re funny—all those things—as long as they get to do anything they want. If you try to stop them from eating your kitchen cabinets or shredding your mattress, it’s over. Not to mention that yes, they wash their food in bowls. But, what most people don’t know is that they also poop in those bowls. 

They make terrible pets. Enjoy them in the wild and leave them alone.

I’ll also add this: 

Raccoons are one of the Big Four rabies vector species. In most of Europe, they’re considered such a dangerous invasive species that they can be hunted, trapped, or shot on sight. In most U.S. states, you need a special permit to own one. 

They also have a high incidence of what’s known as the “raccoon roundworm,” which can be transmitted to dogs and people. In people, it invades the brain and can cause all kinds of neurological symptoms, including death. Children are at most risk, because transmission is from fecal contamination of soil, i.e., putting dirty fingers in mouths. 

Fortunately, human deaths from raccoon rabies or roundworm are rare, but please don’t push it by trying to make one a pet.

text_content: That's how I got my cocktail. He was to loud for my sister and due to her health it got on her nerves. I loved him anyway. I bought my mom a Quaker parrot and he refused to speak in front of me. He eventually slipped up and I finally heard him talk because he didn't know I was in the house. I laughed and I watched him stretch his neck trying to see where I was at. He immediately shut up and didn't say anything until I left the room. He used to go for my feet trying to bite me through my shoes. I asked the woman I got him from about his home life. She had another bird that was a female cockatial so I told my mom he hated me because I took him away from his girlfriend. Such good times. He finally passed and since it was winter and the ground was frozen I wrapped him in a towel, put him in a ziplock bag and placed him in the freezer until we could give him a proper burial. I always wanted another bird but with my two cats I think it would traumatize the bird. I just enjoy my wild birds.

text_content: Sanctuaries are filled with cockatoos. They look fun and snuggly in videos, but they can be unpredictable, aggressive, and LOUD, and I mean hearing loss loud. If there's a falcon within a half mile of my house, everyone within a 2 mile radius will know about it. And that beak is no joke, it can bite through blocks of wood AND your face. So many people don't understand this prior to getting one. That said, they are amazing characters if you commit to changing your life to accommodate them, which includes living with the noise. video_title: 26 Years Caged: Louie the Macaw’s Rescue Story
video_description: In this heartfelt conversation, we sit down with Kelsey from @NoPlucksGiven to hear the incredible rescue story of Louie, ...
text_content:
I'm currently turning my bed room into her (Aphrodite's/afros) room.
I'm looking for ideas tho.
Ive stock piled enough thick card to build a massive frame for perches climbing...  She will have her own door access to the living room via the top "windows"? Above the UK doors.
Things I've covered, TV, days of macaw videos, kinder eggs for treats and training (hasn't chewed yet)
She isn't a fan of water, but she's got better since she's been at a rescue I volunteer at. She's also eased up on her aggression towards woman, most there are woman, and the treat her... Still working on her aggression tho.
On a plus, our bond is strong, she hated the harness before we got it near her....water,woman and harness...umm what happened in her past?? So I started taking her out at night to see the snow, then across the road to the chippy for " hots" lol.
In all the time she's been outside she's been spooked twice, rained on once and baited as we got to my brothers who she loves, that last time was the only time I had to climb a tree to show her what saws do.... Small branch down, then hers... She saw what I was doing and she fell for it and came back to hand lmao.. 
Yes, she's smart... In trouble? Count 1,2....and she's good.
Tell a joke or use sarcasm? She's first to laugh ???? Tone I'm guessing but ....from wild corvids to a macaw was an amazing step. This spring she should meet squeek, a rook who's partner I helped along with a young female rook. He was put down due to injury but squeek has stayed close ever since, last spring she showed a crow my feeding spots going to work... (robins and jackdaw nesting area)
.
.
Its amazing how many things go on day to day.
Sorry for rambling on

Its sounds like you talked to the teacher about changing the diet and cage conditions. I do hope they listen to you. It was nice of the parent to offer to do this, but how many more years before another parent also offers. I dunno exactly HOW much pro bono work you do. But maybe this is also an opportunity to educate others? do you maybe plan to keep up with the turtle? Maybe you could go in once a year as like a "guest" and part of the class that day might be you grooming the turtle in front of the children while educating them a little bit about birds and reptiles? prolly not worth it, you having such a busy schedule and all, unless the school was willing to pay your grooming fee. lol. Still, the teacher might be up for that once a year. 

I had someone bring in a butterfly chrysalis to my first grade class room and educate us about butterflies once, and then they left us the chrysalis so we could all watch it hatch, come time. text_content: Nice video Julie. I had two yellow belly sliders that I ended up releasing because they got too big but don’t worry they knew how to hunt, they ate all of my fish. video_description: Most people automatically recommend ball pythons as the best beginner snake. But after years of keeping both species, many ...
text_content:
My first snake's a rosy boa and he's wonderful; docile just like his rainforest relative, he eats readily, and every time I take him out he's enthusiastically exploring. But I also need to state that I'm in Canada where rosy boas are *extremely difficult to find because almost nobody breeds them here* and that it took me years to find him. They are RARE snakes here! Here in Canada, there's *nothing* common about my little stripey boy!

Meanwhile, Boa imperator is stupidly illegal in my province, so I couldn't adopt Churro's big cousin if I wanted to. And I didn't want to because right now I still find the scale of even adult ball pythons intimidating. Meanwhile I can scoop up Churro one handed, and because he's smaller, it's easier for me to give him an epic enclosure that is much bigger than his species' stated minimum.

text_content: Hello Peoples, I'm currently watching this video and wanted to add some info on salmonella.  I have 3 snakes currently and have never got Salmonella from them.  However I used to volunteer at a reptile center which had hundreds of animals and it was almost like a sauna there sometimes. My first week I was helping to feed snakes and was sweating profusely. As I wiped my face with my hands which had some spilled water on them from carrying a container full of rats in warm water. Some of my sweat went into my mouth and I got Salmonella.   I had a severe case twice because the first round of antibiotics didn't eradicate the salmonella from my body.  So Christmas day (of course) it came back with a vengeance and I ended up in the hospital.   Anyways, I likely got it worse as I take stomach acid suppressing medication which allows the bug to proliferate more.  Point being is to be more careful handling rats and snake food then the snakes themselves.  Also most people who get salmonella usually experience a stomach bug that's not too serious.   I believe my medication made me far more vulnerable to having a severe case.  I hope this helps some of you who are concerned.  Either way it's always a good idea to wash your hands afterwards.  For snakes, salmonella is found in there fecal matter and has no effect on the snakes themselves 😊

text_content:
For me it seems to be a myth that snakes only can digest food well if they are sitting on their hotspot.
I have had my ball python for three years , she is totally healthy (said my snake specialist vet) , eats well, is at a weight of 1600 gram and she never ever digests on her hotspot.
When she was a baby she spent a lot of time on her hot spot but after like 6 months she started to visit it less and less.
She always digests her meals at a spot with a temperature of 25 to 26 degrees Celsius / 77 to 78.8 degrees.
First I was really worried.
But as she is totally fine I got used to it.

video_title: What Happens If A Dog Loses Its Whiskers 😨
text_content:
I remember when I saw a dog owner and he told me that whiskers were useless and came from aging.
The owner lived near my house so I saw what happened. He took his dog outside to play. He told me that his dog thought there was a squirrel climbing up a tree so the dog ran across the street and got run over. He died 2 days later.

Whiskers are really useful for dogs cats or foxes, so removing them causes major issues.
